The spine of Kyagos—a brutal mountain range that defeats casual crossing. Peaks here reach above the treeline into permanent snow, with passes that close entirely during storm season. The range runs roughly northeast to southwest, creating a natural barrier between the northern forests and the southern jungles.

The mountains hold Kyagos's true wealth: veins of silver, copper, and iron laced through the stone, pockets of gemstones that made the island's reputation, and deeper deposits that Drasnian miners have barely begun to tap. Mining operations cluster around the more accessible valleys, with the most lucrative shafts controlled by whichever city-state can hold them.

Travel through the Aktarvkus requires either expensive guides who know the passes, or acceptance that you're probably going to die. The Sylthik city-states maintain a few fortified routes between them, but these are jealously guarded and subject to tolls that make sea travel look economical despite Kyagos's dangerous coasts.

name: The Blades
content: A collection of islands off the southwest coast. Chavux, a green dragon, lairs on one of the northern islands and terrorizes ships passing through the strait.

name: The Breathing Pools
content:
A cluster of hot springs in eastern Blyss, where geothermal vents heat the water to near-boiling. Steam rises constantly, creating a microclimate of fog and warmth that cuts through the usual humidity. The pools "breathe"—water levels rise and fall in slow cycles that take hours to complete, as pressure builds and releases in the underground system.

The pools are sacred to local tribes, used for coming-of-age rituals and the treatment of certain illnesses. Outsiders are not welcome. The last expedition to reach the Breathing Pools—a Shyonan scholarly party in 3,289 SD—was found three weeks later, bones scattered across a wide area, their notes and specimens vanished.

name: The Central Hills
content:
Unnamed hills rise in Azawahasi's central region, breaking up the flat expanse of the surrounding grassland. The hills aren't dramatic—rolling terrain rather than peaks—but they provide valuable variety: sheltered camps during storms, vantage points for spotting game, and reliable springs that don't dry up in the hot season.

Several important seasonal camps cluster in the central hills. Tribes converge here during certain times of year, making the hills a natural meeting ground where news is exchanged, marriages are arranged, and inter-tribal disputes are settled.

name: The Citadel
content:
At the heart of the Carcass Hills stands the ruin of an ancient city-state, known simply as The Citadel. The original name—if it had one—has been lost.

The Citadel was built on and into the largest hill, its walls and towers constructed from the same white limestone as the surrounding terrain. From a distance, the ruins blend into the hillside; only on approach do the worked stones become distinguishable from natural formations.

The city was substantial. Outer walls enclose perhaps two square miles; interior structures suggest a population of 20,000 or more at its height. The architecture is unfamiliar—geometric, angular, with an emphasis on vertical structures that seems designed for beings who could fly or didn't mind climbing. Windows are placed too high for convenient access. Doorways are narrow. Staircases spiral upward with steps uncomfortably tall for human legs.

The Citadel has been abandoned for at least 3,000 years, possibly much longer. The walls still stand in many sections, testament to the quality of the original construction, but roofs have collapsed and interior chambers have filled with debris. What remains above ground has been picked clean by centuries of cautious treasure seekers.

name: The Coinspine Mountains
content: The mountain range along Watar's western border. Named by Dwarblin merchants who saw the peaks as a spine of silver waiting to be mined—and mined it they did. The mountains yield iron, copper, and enough precious metals to fund the trading families' ambitions.

name: The Crags of Geth
content: Mountain range along the western border, separating Erasnus from Griselia and the goblin city-states beyond. The passes are well-traveled despite (or because of) the goblins' constant theatrical threats of invasion.

name: The Croaking Briars
content:
The living thorn-barrier separating Trømgar from Phirexes deserves special mention. This is not natural vegetation—the briars are responsive, aggressive, and seemingly intelligent. They grow toward warmth and blood, their hooked thorns designed to catch and hold.

The Strømgodden have attacked the briars many times, seeking passage to whatever lies beyond. Each assault fails. The briars regenerate overnight, growing thicker and more tangled where blood has been spilled. Older dwarves speak of a time before the briars existed, when traffic between the regions was possible, but that time is generations past.

Safe passages through the briars exist, but they shift weekly and are known only to those with local knowledge—traders, smugglers, and people who have made accommodations the Strømgodden consider dishonorable.

name: The Darkened Coast
content:
East of the Taflex Forest and south of Grimreach, along the northwest coast of the Agreben Sea, lies the Darkened Coast—a stretch of shoreline perpetually shrouded in shadow. The effect is not meteorological; clouds and fog play no part. The light simply fails to reach full strength here, as if something is drinking it.

The waters teem with dark-attuned sea creatures: shadow rays that glide beneath the waves, void eels that drain light from their surroundings, and worse things that rise from the lightless depths during moonless nights. Sailors avoid this coast when possible, as ships that venture too close risk being pulled under by creatures that exist more in shadow than flesh.

The Darkened Coast's nature predates King Kasper's curse, but scholars note the shadow has deepened since. Whether the curse awakened something already present, or whether the concentrated death-energy fed it, remains debated.

name: The Dragon Shadow
content:
Northwest of Thespia lies Morgnor's Cradle—the mountain territory of Morgnor, King of the Mountains. The Hills of Red Gold and Mt. Kazlos mark the edge of his domain. Thespia has always paid tribute and kept a small army to avoid his wrath.

Queen Akta has not continued the tribute. The dragon has not yet responded.

name: The Drift
content:
The most treacherous terrain in the Jungles of Godahi. The Drift is not a swamp in the traditional sense but a region where the land itself seems unstable—floating islands of matted vegetation drift on deeper water, channels appear and disappear, and the boundary between solid ground and drowning-depth liquid is impossible to determine.

The Drift earned its name from the constant motion of its surface. A path that exists in the morning may be gone by afternoon. Travelers who survive the crossing describe a surreal landscape where the horizon shifts and the ground moves beneath their feet.

Despite its dangers, The Drift must be crossed to reach certain passes through the Cloud Mountains. Those seeking to travel south have few alternatives—the routes around The Drift add weeks to the journey.

name: The Face
content:
Tags: cliffs, geography, landmark

A massive cliff formation rising between West Whitewall and East Whitewall, forming the central portion of Venalthier's northern ice barrier. The Face takes its name from a natural rock formation visible in the cliff that resembles a colossal human visage—though whether it is truly natural remains a matter of debate among those few who have seen it.

name: The Firespine
content:
A jagged fissure tears through the Nekanzi Jungle from north to south, following the path of the Yolus fire leyline where it runs close to the surface. The Neka call it the Firespine because it resembles exactly that—the exposed vertebrae of something colossal and long-dead, glowing with residual heat.

The fissure varies in width from a few feet to gaps wide enough to swallow a house. Smoke seeps from the narrower sections. The wider gaps exhale gouts of flame at irregular intervals, and the rock along the edges glows dull red even in daylight. The temperature near the Firespine runs twenty degrees hotter than the surrounding jungle, and the air tastes of sulfur and hot stone.

Fire spirits emerge here. Not constantly, but frequently enough that the Neka have developed an entire tradition around managing them. Most are minor elementals—flickering humanoid shapes, serpents of living flame, floating motes of malicious heat. Occasionally something larger pulls itself from the fissure. The Neka don't discuss those incidents with outsiders.

The leyline's power makes fire magic significantly more potent within a mile of the Firespine. Neka fire-speakers can accomplish workings here that would exhaust them elsewhere. Foreign pyromancers who've visited (the few who were permitted) describe it as intoxicating—and dangerous. The power comes easily, but it doesn't want to stop.

name: The Freedom Mountains
content:
The central mountain range bisecting Pesalolo, running the full length of the island. The name is bitterly ironic or desperately hopeful depending on who uses it—the mountains are both the barrier that prevents Gorath from simply conquering the north, and the escape route for slaves fleeing the southern coast.

The range comprises several named sections:
- **Apuindi Mountains** — The eastern section, rising from the coast near Divinity Passage
- **Gesichi Mountains** — The central section, where Slavewatch fortress commands the major passes
- **Tishkung Mountains** — The southwestern section, descending toward the Ryujilin Hills

The mountains are high enough to catch clouds, keeping the slopes perpetually damp and the passes frequently fogged. Gorathi military expeditions have repeatedly failed to establish permanent control of the highland routes—the terrain favors those who know it, and the Drasnian dwarves have known it for millennia.

name: The Fungal Gardens
content:
An area in western Blyss where bioluminescent fungi have colonized several square miles of forest floor. At night (not that day and night differ much here), the gardens glow in shifting patterns of blue, green, and sickly yellow. The effect is beautiful from a distance. Up close, it's nauseating—the spores interfere with balance and perception, causing disorientation that builds over hours into full hallucinations.

The Ix'Meglyakuk harvest certain fungi from the gardens for medicinal and recreational purposes. They know which species are safe, which are poisonous, and which unlock visions that might be prophetic or might be the prelude to death. They do not explain the distinctions to outsiders.

name: The Glog
content:
Tags: forest, geography, pelera

A vast expanse of dense, waterlogged forest sprawling across the northern Dragon's Spine coast between the open sea and the mountains. The Glog earned its name from the sound boots make in its perpetually sodden ground—a wet, sucking noise that accompanies every step. The forest is cold, dark, and deeply unwelcoming, but it's also one of the few relatively flat, accessible regions on this stretch of coast.

name: The Gnomestone Mountains
content:
The mountain range that defines Joswik's southern border, named for the gnome communities that supposedly dwelt here before humans arrived. Whether gnomes ever lived here is debatable; the stone formations that look like gnome-carved structures might just be unusual geology.

What's not debatable is the wealth. Iron and copper seams run through these mountains, and mining towns dot the valleys and passes. The mountain lords have always been semi-independent, paying taxes to whoever rules in Padena but governing their own affairs.

name: The Greenway
content:
The main road connecting Enymu to Tarkhetan and the rest of Tarkhon. Well-maintained by Enymese standards (which means passable but not paved), the Greenway carries most of the grain, wool, and livestock that flows from Enymu to Nektuna's markets.

Traveling the Greenway, you pass through the heart of Enymu's farmland: villages every few miles, fields stretching to the horizon, the occasional fortified manor house where local lords administer their lands.

name: The Grey Mountains
content: The sub-range of the Kharvorn where Myorna is situated. The mountains contain extensive underground complexes—remnants of thousands of years of subterranean civilization. Many passages remain unexplored or sealed.

name: The Guiles
content:
Tags: forest, geography, illusion

The Guiles is a dense forest wedged between the Hellion Hills to the west and the Twaan Forests to the north, stretching south toward Kobuk. It takes its name from the experience of traveling through it: the forest lies, and those who trust their senses here find themselves hopelessly lost.

No one is entirely certain why. The leading theories involve residual magic from the nearby ley lines, some property of the local flora, or the influence of creatures that prefer confused prey. The Guiles isn't actively malicious—people who stay calm and patient usually find their way out eventually—but it makes navigation nearly impossible through conventional means.

name: The Highlands
content:
Between the river valleys, the highlands rise in gentle swells of savanna grass. These uplands are sparsely populated by human standards, but they're where the Rentar have made their home.

**Rentar Monasteries**

Scattered across the highlands are dozens of Rentar monasteries—stone compounds built for contemplation, martial training, and spiritual communion. Each monastery maintains gardens, livestock, and training grounds sufficient for self-sufficiency. They trade with the valley towns but could survive without them.

The largest concentration of monasteries lies between the Stulov and Riki valleys, in the highlands south of Yeletity. This region has become informally known as the **Holy Hills**, though the name makes human residents uncomfortable—it feels presumptuous, like the Rentar are claiming the land's soul.

**The Pyramid of Five Sides**

In the highlands between the Chechik and Stulov valleys, southeast of Pyadrov. See the full entry for details. The Pyramid predates human settlement—a stepped structure whose fifth face exists only in Celestia. Pilgrims come from across Aboyinzu to meditate in its shadow.

name: The Hill Roads
content:
A network of trails winds through Belanora, connecting the scattered settlements and linking the coast to the interior. These paths are unmarked and unmapped—knowing them requires local guidance or years of personal experience. Outsiders who attempt to navigate Belanora without help typically get lost, sometimes fatally.

The most important route is the **Mourner's Path**, which connects Tearfall on Belanorn Bay to the edge of Locquine territory. Traders and explorers use this trail to approach the pixie city-state while minimizing their time on the Misery River itself. It takes three days on foot, winding through the hill passes before descending into the jungle.

name: The Honor River System
content:
The **Honor River** rises in the eastern highlands and flows westward through Avalon's heart, eventually emptying into Munari Inlet. Its valley contains the kingdom's best farmland and most of its population.

**Honor Creek** branches northeast from the main river, draining the Pathanul region. The creek's waters are said to reveal glimpses of the future to those who drink from certain pools—though the priests warn that unguided visions bring madness more often than wisdom.

**Honor Moss** is the marshy region where the river slows near Tarolin, spreading into wetlands thick with reeds and waterfowl. The Moss produces medicinal herbs used in time-magic rituals.

**Pale Run** flows through the southern forests, eventually joining the river systems that drain toward Chimea.

name: The Kettle
content:
Kettle Bay itself is a broad, sheltered inlet—natural harbor formed where the coastline curves inward. The "kettle" name comes from the bay's shape and the way storms can trap ships inside, churning the water.

The bay's waters are productive fishing grounds, and Jüt's fishing fleet is substantial. Salted fish is one of the few goods Jüt actually produces rather than just trading.

name: The Knulak Lakes
content:
The Knulak Lakes are a cluster of interconnected bodies of water fed by glacial melt from the Qynxis Mountains to the south. The lakes remain unfrozen for much of the year, warmed by geothermal springs that bubble up from below. This creates a microclimate around the lakes where the forest grows thicker and the winters are marginally more survivable.

The lakes are rich with fish—a crucial food source for the scattered communities of the region. Fishing rights are loosely respected among the various families and small clans who live here.

name: The Lizard's Back
content: A geographic feature along the southern border with Zintacas—a ridge or rocky formation marking the edge of Stipen's forests.

name: The Ofrenia Passage
content:
The strait between Paluva Amati and Ofrenia, connecting the Gulf of Joswik to the open Middle Sea. Control of the Passage has always been contested—now that Alisandra holds both Sylke and Tornia, she effectively controls both ends. Joswik's few ships and the island's fishing boats are the only traffic she doesn't command.

The Passage is dangerous in storms; ships shelter in Shipton's harbor until weather clears. This forced hospitality is Myastor's primary source of legitimate income—provisioning, repairs, and entertainment for bored sailors waiting out bad seas.

name: The Oshkensi Plateau
content: A vast grassland plateau in the southwestern interior, rising above the surrounding valleys like a table set for giants. The herders who live here raise sheep and cattle in numbers that feed much of Ofrenia—or did, before the war disrupted everything.

name: The Passage
content: The strait separating Joswik from Tornia to the north, and from Myastor's island to the east. The Passage has always been contested water—whoever controls it controls traffic between Ofrenia's kingdoms. With Alisandra holding Tornia, the Passage has become a flashpoint. Joswik's few naval vessels patrol nervously, waiting for Sylke's fleet to make a move.

name: The Pin Isles
content:
The northern boundary of Iqes Sound is marked by the Pin Isles — a chain of narrow, pointed islands stretching between Iqes and Gyosha. The isles are named for their distinctive shapes: vertical spires of rock that jut from the water like pins stuck in a cushion.

The Pin Isles mark the boundary between Iqes's waters and Gyosha's sphere of influence. Fishing rights in the channels between the pins are contested; Iqes fishermen and Mirko-affiliated boats from Gyosha regularly clash over the best grounds. These disputes rarely escalate beyond thrown punches and cut nets — neither side wants to start a real conflict — but tensions simmer constantly.

The isles themselves are uninhabited. The rock is too steep for settlement, the soil nonexistent, the exposure too brutal. But the channels between them are excellent fishing grounds, and the spires make useful landmarks for navigation. Several of the larger pins have been marked with painted symbols — territorial claims, navigation aids, or warnings, depending on who you ask.

name: The Purlin Hills
content:
Running north-south through the heart of Titania, the Purlin Hills provide the closest thing the jungle has to highlands. The terrain rises a few hundred feet above the lowland average—not mountains by any measure, but enough elevation to break the endless canopy and allow some respite from the crushing humidity.

The hills are Gorath's most productive logging region. The slopes grow slower-growing, harder wood than the lowlands, and the slightly drier conditions make year-round operations possible. Logging camps cluster throughout the Purlins, connected by trails that require constant clearing to remain passable.

The hills also serve as rough territory for anything trying to move overland. Gorathi criminals, escaped slaves, and others fleeing authority often head for the Purlins, where the broken terrain and dense forest make pursuit difficult. The legions maintain bounty hunters who specialize in tracking through the hills—a dangerous and profitable profession.

name: The River of Petals
content:
The most notable waterway in the Cerulean Crests is the River of Petals, which rises from springs in the central range and flows south toward the Dalizi lowlands. The river gets its name from the flower petals that cover its surface during spring bloom—the copper-rich soils of the Crests support unique alpine flowers that shed their petals into the streams.

The river valley provides the easiest route through the Cerulean Crests, connecting the highland interior to the Dalizi Confederation. This is the primary southern access point to the highlands, less difficult than the Titan Stairs and less dangerous than the Chalaari Valley's proximity to the ogre-infested Troyan Mountains.

name: The Salt Moors
content: Salt marshes in the lowlands, source of preserving salt for the fishing industry.

name: The Screech
content:
Tags: mountains, geography, wind

A jagged range of peaks separating The Glog from the eastern coastline, named for the constant, keening sound the wind makes as it tears through the exposed rock formations. The Screech is not particularly tall by Dragon's Spine standards, but its geology is distinctive—countless spires, arches, and wind-carved channels that transform every breeze into an eerie symphony of whistles, moans, and shrieks.

name: The Screech Peaks (Ios Side)
content:
The southwestern slopes of the Screech Peaks define Ios's northern boundary. The mountains provide natural defenses, mineral resources, and the headwaters of the Dipindre River. The passes through to Moigos are few and difficult, limiting contact between the states.

Todakres is built against these mountains, using their height for defense and their stone for construction. The upper reaches above the city are wild — home to the creatures that give the Screech Peaks their name.

name: The Silwyn River
content:
The lifeblood of the region. It rises from springs in the southern highlands, flows north through the heart of the plains, and crosses into Iylovia—where it becomes the water the Gyv depend on to survive.

The Silzar could dam it. They could end the war in a season.

They haven't. Not yet. The Mammoth Covenant holds water sacred, and the elders refuse to cross that line. But the younger generation is asking harder questions every year.

name: The Stone Forest
content:
The western border between Enymu and Nektuna is marked by one of Alaria's great mysteries: an entire forest transformed into stone.

Every tree stands petrified in perfect detail—bark, branches, leaves, all gray stone. Animals caught in the transformation remain frozen: birds mid-flight, deer mid-leap, a bear eternally rising on its hind legs. The forest is silent except for wind through stone branches.

**The Forbidden Zone**: Entry to the Stone Forest is forbidden by ancient pact between Enymu and Nektuna. No one remembers exactly when the pact was made or why. Everyone knows the rule: stay out.

**The Titan Crystals**: In the roots of the stone trees grow crystalline formations that ring at a frequency only titans can hear. If disturbed by sentient touch, these crystals might wake the **Hykravones**—slumbering titans buried deep beneath the earth.

**The Wardens**: Enymu's Stone Wardens patrol the forest's borders, turning away anyone who approaches. They don't enter the forest themselves. They just make sure no one else does.

**The Pressure**: Scholars from Tarkhetan want access. They call the pact "superstition." High Warden Gellert and Lord Councillor Beren have refused. But King Selron has been pressuring King Aldric, and Aldric trusts Selron...

name: The Swallowing Mire
content:
A vast bog in southern Blyss where the boundary between land and water dissolves entirely. The Swallowing Mire extends for miles in every direction—a maze of stagnant pools, floating vegetation mats, and stretches of ground that look solid until you step on them. The water is dark, acidic, and home to things that pull prey under.

The mire marks the southern edge of Draphilir's claimed territory. Beyond it lies the transition zone between Blyss and the central Beastlands, and eventually the approaches to Disaster Passage. Crossing the mire is possible with guides; without them, it's suicide.

name: The Tarns
content:
A region of highland lakes and pools in northeastern Mueras, fed by rainfall from the Mueras Highlands and streams descending from the Dustwind Plateau. The tarns provide the primary freshwater source for the northern settlements—a fact that has made them contested territory throughout Mueran history.

Currently, the tarns fall under the loose protection of House Hammren, though several smaller merchant families maintain estates here. The freshwater fisheries and the waterfowl hunting draw wealthy Muerans seeking respite from the salt air and political intrigue of the coast.

name: The Twins
content:
Tags: island, geography

TODO

name: The Vokas Enrisikna Range
content: A small mountain range that stretches between the Ocean of Elorinia to the west and the Bejeweled Sea to the east. This range serves as the geographic boundary between the Seacleft Coast and the Farlands to the south.

name: The Whistle-Stone Mountains
content: The mountain range that defines Glorin. Natural caverns and tunnels create an eerie whistling when wind passes through—unsettling to outsiders, sacred to the Glorindians who call it the voice of the mountains.

name: The Wolfmaw
content:
The Wolfmaw is a peninsula extending roughly 30 miles into the bay, split at its tip into two rocky headlands that resemble open jaws—hence the name. The formation creates a natural breakwater, sheltering the inner bay from the worst ocean swells.

The peninsula's terrain is rougher than the mainland forest—rocky outcrops interrupt the tree cover, and the soil is thin enough that the canopy never fully closes. This relative openness makes the Wolfmaw marginally safer than the interior forest, though "safer" is relative. Ulvsjael packs use the peninsula as hunting ground, and the broken terrain provides excellent ambush positions.

The Wolfmaw's southern shore faces open ocean and catches the brunt of storms rolling in from the west. Shipwrecks dot the coastline, their timbers and cargo scattered across the rocks. Salvage is possible but risky—the noise of working a wreck attracts attention from the forest.

name: Thraken Lake
content:
Tags: lake, geography

A lake in the Dragon's Spine Coast.

TODO

name: Thrallwood
content:
The western slopes of the Phalkrene Mountains drain into Thrallwood—a swamp-forest that stretches to Shamerei Bay. The trees here grow from standing water, their roots tangled into vast platforms of rotting vegetation. Mist rises perpetually from the warm, stagnant pools.

Something old claims Thrallwood. Not a god, not a spirit—something from the same age as the titan whose skull became Pyonokraus. Perhaps it served that titan once. Perhaps it was the titan's enemy. Whatever it was, it bound this place to its will, and that binding has outlasted the binder.

Every creature in Thrallwood serves. The beasts move in patterns too regular to be natural, patrolling routes they've walked for centuries. Birds call at precise intervals. Insects swarm in geometric formations. Even the plants seem to reach toward intruders with coordinated purpose.

The compulsion is contagious. Those who linger in Thrallwood begin to feel the pull—an urge to walk a certain path, to guard a certain tree, to wait for instructions that never come. The longer one stays, the harder it becomes to leave. After a few days, the victim stops wanting to. After a week, they forget they ever wanted anything else.

Thralls wander the forest still: humans, gnomes, beasts, all moving in silent service to a master that may be dead, sleeping, or simply elsewhere. They do not speak. They do not acknowledge outsiders. But they will stop anyone from reaching whatever lies at Thrallwood's heart.

name: Thunderdark Peaks
content:
Tags: mountains, geography

A rugged mountain range in the eastern Inner Rim, home to the Strømgodden dwarves of Trømgar. The peaks are named for the violent thunderstorms that gather around them with eerie regularity—lightning flashes illuminate the dark granite ridges, and thunder echoes through the valleys like the war-drums of ancient gods.

name: Tiber's Passage
content: The strait south of Erpeus, between the island and Tykos to the south. The waters here are busy with traffic moving between Erpeus, Tykos, and Ofrenia.

name: Timeless Wastes
content:
Southwest of Blyzrynthine and west of Giantfell lie the Timeless Wastes—a frozen desert where time itself behaves strangely. Travelers cannot keep track of time accurately here; hours feel like days, days feel like hours, and aging progresses at unpredictable rates. Some emerge having aged decades in what felt like a day, while others spend what seems like weeks only to find mere hours have passed in the outside world. Everything about the Wastes feels wrong and disorienting, as if time itself has come unmoored from reality.

The River of Swords flows through the western Wastes—named not for any battle but for the way ice crystals form along its banks in blade-like formations that can slice through leather and flesh.

name: Tirexonos
content:
The capital, such as it is. Tirexonos sprawls across a cluster of islands where the Jiji River splits into multiple channels, its buildings raised on stilts above the floodwaters. This is the seat of whatever passes for central government in Nashua—the Council of Fang and Scale, where clan leaders gather to coordinate the endless war.

The city's architecture is distinctive: curving walls that echo serpentine movement, buildings that seem to grow organically from the terrain, and a maze-like layout that would trap invaders in killing zones. The central island holds the Temple of the Hunt, where the greatest Ix'Vasyla warriors are honored and where prisoners of particular importance are... processed.

Foreign visitors are rare but not unknown. Tirexonos maintains a small quarter for diplomats and merchants, heavily watched and easily isolated. The message is clear: you are here on sufferance.

name: Tishkung Mountains
content:
Tags: mountains, geography

Mountains on Pesalolo.

TODO

name: Titan Stairs
content: The **Titan Stairs** are the western gateway to the Dalizi Highlands, a dramatic stepped formation where the land rises from the lowland Dalizi Confederation toward the mountain wall in a series of massive terraces. Each "step" is a plateau tens of miles across, separated by cliffs and escarpments that must be climbed or circumvented. The name refers to the scale—the formation looks like stairs built for beings far larger than humans.

name: Tongueroot Forest
content: A forest in the Dalizi Wildlands.

name: Tonyfall River
content: The major river system running through South Suki, navigable for much of its length. Aldaville and Frowat control key points along its course. The river serves as the primary transportation route for jungle products heading to market.

name: Toothlar Mountains
content: The **Toothlar Mountains** form the southwestern boundary of the Dalizi Highlands, a jagged wall of peaks that separates the highland interior from the lowland approaches. The name refers to the range's profile—from the Dalizi plains, the peaks look like a row of broken teeth against the sky, sharp and irregular and vaguely threatening.

name: Tora Mazîl
content:
The heart of Qet Yemani—a vast elevated forest covering the central and northern portions of the nation. Tora Mazîl sits on a subtle plateau above the surrounding jungle, creating a distinct ecosystem. The trees here grow even taller than elsewhere in the Jungles, with some specimens exceeding 200 feet. The forest floor is more open than typical jungle, allowing easier travel but also harboring different dangers.

Tora Mazîl is sacred to the people of Qet Yemani. Ancient groves within the forest are sites of religious significance, and the harvest of certain tree species is strictly regulated by traditional law. The forest produces some of the most valuable hardwoods in Alaria—dense, beautifully grained timber that commands premium prices in distant markets.

name: Torgan Passage
content:
Tags: strait, geography

TODO

name: Torguli Islands
content:
Tags: island, geography

A long, curving chain of islands stretching southwest from the main Tyvern archipelago into open water. The Torguli Islands arc across perhaps a hundred miles of sea, each island visible from the next, forming a natural pathway that sailors have followed since before recorded history.

The islands are uninhabited in any permanent sense. They're too small, too exposed, and too far from the resources needed for sustained settlement. But their chain-like arrangement makes them invaluable for navigation — following the Torguli will take you southwest toward deeper waters, and each island serves as a waypoint confirming you're still on course.

name: Traitor Bay
content:
Tags: bay, geography

A bay within Chimea.

TODO

name: Triuci
content:
Tags: region, giants

A hilly region in the northern Sandreach Mountains, home to hill giant clans known throughout the region for their aggression and brutality. The Triuci giants are smaller than their southern cousins in Malo Kon Greikh but make up for it with numbers, viciousness, and a complete lack of restraint.

name: Troyan Mountains
content: The **Troyan Mountains** form the southern wall of the Dalizi Highlands, a range of broken peaks and deep valleys that separates the highland interior from the lowlands of the Dalizi Confederation. Named for an ancient Dalizi hero who allegedly died fighting giants in these peaks, the mountains are best known today for the ogre clans that make the Ogre Hills their home.

name: Tsam'iynu
content:
Tags: citystate

gnome theocracy. Inhabited by foxbourne gnomes, celibacy, knights, flowing robes, flower knights

name: Tugachi
content:
Tags: forest, geography

A forest within Shyona.

TODO

name: Tukiwood
content:
Tags: forest, geography, ve, sestros

The southern forest of Sestros, marking the transition between the kingdom's settled grasslands and the wilder country beyond. Tukiwood is temperate woodland—dense enough to provide timber and game, open enough that the Shailin don't find it threatening.

name: Turbuni
content: A city-state in Dalizi.

name: Tuur (Capital)
content:
Population: ~3,000

The capital sits where the Sova Chyda emerges from the Winterwood, commanding both the river trade and access to the sacred forest. Tuur is older than the split—it served as the summer residence of the unified kingdom's monarchs, who wintered in what is now Klevnaf-controlled territory. That history makes Istora's claim to legitimacy complicated; they hold the summer palace but lost the ancestral seat.

Queen Lamenrae rules from the Palace of Seasons, a graceful complex of white stone towers connected by covered bridges. The architecture reflects centuries of wealth and peace now interrupted. Recent construction—walls reinforced, towers converted to watchtowers—shows the war's impact.

Tuur's population has swelled with refugees from the contested hills and elves who chose Lamenrae's faction when the kingdom split. Housing is tight; tempers are short.

name: Tyberoskos
content:
South of the Pearly Mountains, where the land descends toward the coast, Tyberoskos spreads across rolling hills of limestone and clay. This is not a forest that welcomes travelers. The ground is treacherous—riddled with sinkholes that open without warning, swallowing the unwary into darkness. The trees have adapted with vast root systems that grip the unstable earth, their canopy so thick that the forest floor exists in perpetual twilight.

Two peoples call Tyberoskos home, bound together by necessity and lunar rhythm.

name: Tykon Channel
content: The waters east of Erpeus and west of Ofrenia, separating Erpeus from the three kingdoms' ongoing civil war. Traffic through the Tykon Channel has declined since Alisandra's conquest disrupted regional trade.

name: Tyror Miig
content:
Tags: plains, geography, rakite

The northern grasslands of Rakite—summer territory for the buffalo herds and the tribes that follow them. "Tyror Miig" means "high grass" in the Rakiten tongue, and the name is accurate: the grass here grows taller than anywhere else in the plains, sometimes reaching eight feet in good years.

name: Tyror Weklis
content:
Tags: plains, geography, rakite

The southern grasslands of Rakite—winter territory where the herds shelter from the cold winds that sweep down from the north. "Tyror Weklis" means "sleeping grass" in the Rakiten tongue, referring to the way the plains go dormant in winter, the tall stalks bending under frost and snow.

name: Ulbrizil
content:
Tags: city, state, nation

A city-state in Malo Kon Greikh.

TODO

name: Umlas
content:
The holy capital rises from the central plains where the original covenant was struck. Seven great temples—one for each patron—ring a central plaza dominated by the Hall of Convergence where the Hierophancy meets. The Temple of Theros faces east, catching the dawn; the Temple of Petranos is half-buried, its lower levels extending deep into the earth; the Temple of Lykanos stands at the crossroads where the major pilgrimage routes meet.

Umlas isn't the largest city on Urok, but it may be the most orderly. Streets follow a geometric pattern radiating from the central plaza. Buildings are constructed to last centuries. The constant sound of bells marks the hours and calls the faithful to prayer. Visitors describe the city as beautiful but unsettling—too quiet, too clean, too *watched*.

The population hovers around forty thousand, swelling during major festivals when pilgrims arrive from across Xoth. Most residents work in temple administration, craft production, or services for the endless stream of visitors. The truly devout seek positions in Umlas; living in the holy city is itself a form of worship.

name: Üod
content:
Tags: citystate

One of the three dwarven city-states making up First Brotherhood.

name: Üod
content:
Tags: citystate, dwarves, first-brotherhood

One of the three leadership city-states of the First Brotherhood—the ancient dwarven coalition formed when the world was far more dangerous. Along with Blvnird and Melaia, Üod's citizens hold the exclusive right to lead the Brotherhood.

name: Urchin Bay
content:
The large bay south of Oyster Bay, named for the giant urchins that make it treacherous. The waters are deeper, colder, and far more dangerous. Ships that venture in report seeing massive spined shapes in the murky depths—and those are the ships that come back.

Rumors persist of treasure in Urchin Bay—centuries of wrecked ships accumulated on the bottom. Expeditions occasionally form to recover it. Most fail expensively.

name: Urkrakr
content:
Tags: city, ruin

A ruined city-state on the Xeltri Plateau, destroyed by hill giants.

TODO

name: Urzahd
content: The capital, in the northwest where the river from Glorin provides life. Seat of King Salomor's court and home to the finest glass workshops in the world. The nobility live in elegant estates; laborers crowd the outer districts.

name: Vad Ilghak
content: The capital city, built into the Grey Mountains. A vertical city of stone, carved chambers, and passages leading deep underground to the old warrens. Home to the royal palace, the great memory libraries, and the massive Deoric library—one of the largest collections of sacrifice-based magical texts in existence.

name: Valley of Thagrund
content: TODO

name: Valley of the Witches
content:
Tags: valley, geography, dangerous

The Valley of the Witches is a secluded vale in the Morygun Mountains, east of Korgül and northwest of Phaerhom. Its name is earned: the valley is home to a coven of powerful witches who have claimed it for generations, and they do not welcome visitors.

name: Vassum
content:
The northwestern city-state, positioned where Moonwood meets Jungula Serpentine. Vassum has always been Kyagos's window to the outside world—the city-state most engaged with foreign trade, most tolerant of outsiders, most willing to bend the rigid Sylthik social codes when profit was involved.

The trade tower in Syvlius connected to the astral current, but goods still had to reach Syvlius somehow. Vassum controlled the sea routes, the overland passes, the warehouses where foreign cargo accumulated before the mountain crossing. This made them wealthy. It also made them dependent on Syvlius's infrastructure.

Now that infrastructure is gone, and Vassum is scrambling to adapt.

name: Vastilin
content:
The human capital on Mt. Arn. A city of commerce, craft, and comfortable excess.

Vastilin is everything Eynmore isn't—loud, crowded, messy, alive. Markets compete for attention. Foundries pour smoke. Taverns overflow. It's a proper city, even if it's built on a mountainside in the middle of nowhere.

**Architecture**: Practical construction in local stone, expanded piecemeal over centuries. The city has no plan—buildings were added where needed, creating a maze of streets that locals navigate by instinct.

**Government**: The **Merchant Council** of seven major families controls city affairs. The **First Merchant**, currently **Aldous Brennan**, mediates between them and represents Vastilin in joint decisions with Eynmore.

**Character**: Busy. Pragmatic. Focused on the next deal, the next shipment, the next opportunity. Visitors find it refreshingly normal after the strangeness of Rhea culture.

name: Veluminante Forest
content:
Tags: forest, geography, free-isles, xabraedia

The purple-tinged forest that covers much of Xabraedia's interior, spreading outward from Starfall like a slow infection. The Veluminante glows faintly at night, its bioluminescent life forms creating an eerie luminescence that can be seen from ships passing through The Spout. The Velathi of Hik enter the forest regularly; outsiders are strongly advised not to.

name: Vila Bonsa
content:
The capital rises from the central archipelago on wooden platforms and stone foundations driven deep into the muck. Buildings stand on stilts, connected by walkways that flood predictably with the tides. The Alekroin designed it this way. What looks like poor planning to visitors is actually architecture optimized for ambush—the water beneath the walkways is never empty.

The Palace of the Six Jaws dominates the city center, a sprawling complex where the King and the six noble houses conduct their slow, deliberate politics. Royal succession follows an ancient ritual: an egg is selected from the King's brood, incubated at precise temperatures in isolation, watched by keepers who will never see it hatch. The chosen heir grows larger, stronger, and more cunning than any other Alekroin—a process the Alekroin do not explain and outsiders do not understand.

Vila Bonsa's markets trade in swamp goods: cured hides, medicinal plants, bioluminescent algae harvested from the deeper channels, and shellfish in varieties found nowhere else. Metal is the primary import—the swamps offer little ore. Merchants who trade here learn quickly that the Alekroin do not haggle. They state a price. You accept it or you leave.

name: Vog Dridril
content:
Tags: city, state, nation

A city-state in Malo Kon Greikh.

TODO

name: Vokas Enrisikna
content:
Tags: mountains, geography, rakite, farlands

The western mountain barrier of Rakite—a rugged north-south range separating the plains from the Jeh Bli wilderness beyond. The name means "grey wall" in Rakiten, and the description is apt: the mountains present a uniform face of grey stone and sparse vegetation, discouraging passage in either direction.

name: Vokas Pendi
content:
Tags: mountains, geography, rakite

The southern mountain barrier of Rakite—a lower, wider range than Vokas Enrisikna, forming the boundary between the Rakiten plains and the territories beyond. "Pendi" means "horizon" in Rakiten; the mountains are always visible on the southern skyline, a constant landmark for the tribes.

name: Vrommol
content:
Tags: city, state, nation

A city-state in Malo Kon Greikh.

TODO

name: Vykus
content:
Tags: city, state, nation

A city-state in the Innerrim.

TODO

name: Vystril
content:
The capital of Vystrilik, situated in the central-western jungle at the base of the Tambrielle Mountains. Vystril is a prosperous trading city controlling the primary mountain passes. The city's architecture reflects its role as a crossroads—influences from the coast, the jungle interior, and the eastern seaboard blend in its buildings and marketplaces.

The city maintains extensive warehouses where goods are sorted, taxed, and transferred between jungle canoes and the pack trains that cross the mountains.

name: Wastes of Dezolos
content:
Tags: desert, poi

Water magically evaporates here—not just because it is hot. It won't last more than a day, and it can't be created magically.

name: Wer Azenzi
content:
Tags: lake, geography, riin, eyendra, wycendeula

A cluster of small lakes in northern Eyendra, scattered through the forest between Lean D'Riin and the coast. Unlike the lakes of Kama Sa'malina far to the south, Wer Azenzi's waters are tied to the Riin—the ancient civilization that merged with the Eyendra forest two thousand years ago. The lakes are where the Riin presence pools most densely, and that presence makes itself felt.

The name is Riin. No one knows exactly what it means anymore—possibly "gathering waters" or "waters that hold." The translation hardly matters. What matters is that these lakes are not entirely natural, and they are not entirely empty.

name: West Tolkarsus
content:
Tags: river, geography

The northern tributary of the Grand Tolkarsus river system, the West Tolkarsus flows from the highlands north of the Westwilds, passing through the Homdrum Hills before joining with other waterways to form the larger river that eventually reaches the Grieb Lakes and Plenjorn Swamp.

name: Western Lakes
content: Several towns dot the shores of lakes in western Adron. Beneath the waters lie drowned Triton ruins—remnants of the civilization that inhabited this land before fleeing to the Alrock Ocean eons ago.

name: Wetu
content: A city-state in Dalizi.

name: Whisper Bay
content:
Tags: bay, geography

A bay within Meadow Sound.

TODO

name: White Hills
content:
Tags: hills

Home to the Thrygun orcs.

name: Whitefin Bay
content:
The major bay dominating Poum Titch's eastern coast, named for the silver-scaled whitefin fish that have sustained coastal communities for centuries. Whitefin is the primary protein source for most of the nation—smoked, salted, or fresh, it appears on every table.

The war has disrupted the fishing grounds. Naval skirmishes between loyalist and rebel vessels make open-water fishing dangerous. Catches are down. Prices are up. The coastal towns grow hungry, and hungry Scalawags grow desperate.

name: Whitewood
content:
A vast forest of silver-barked trees running north-south along Aal Salma's eastern border, forming a natural boundary with Poum Titch. The wood of these trees is pale, almost white, and prized for shipbuilding—light, strong, and resistant to rot.

Logging camps work the northern reaches, sending timber down to Zamrat Aqabah's shipyards. The deeper portions of the Whitewood remain wild and largely unexplored. Travelers who stray from the main trails report disorientation, strange silences, and the sense of being watched. Most stay on the roads.

The Whitewood has become the primary route for refugees fleeing the Poum Titch civil war. They emerge from the tree line starving and traumatized, with stories of violence that grow worse with each wave.

name: Wisdom Creek
content:
Wisdom Creek flows from the spring at Othyndria (the ruined capital, now home to Rothogomos the cruel dragon) down into the valley between Argysis and Hephake. The river earned its name from the great wisdom that once flowed from the Kings of Argysis when the dwarven kingdom was at its height.

The water flows northeast, eventually reaching Enros Lake, a large lake just south of the Jotun Hills—a mountain range stretching east-west from Winterwood in the west to the southwest edge of Konai Mora.

name: Wisgarrd
content:
A dwarven city-state inland from Maun, built into the foothills where the Pearly Mountains descend toward the coast. Wisgarrd is home to the School of Jewelry, where master craftsmen teach the art of gem-cutting and, to select students, the secrets of magical enchantment. See the full state entry for details on its curriculum, the legendary Book of Gems, and its complicated relationship with Maun.

The Peonie River flows past Wisgarrd's walls, its banks thick with the flowering plants that give the river its name. Jewelers draw inspiration from the blooms; some say no Wisgarrd piece is complete without at least one element borrowed from the peonie's form.

name: Witchbreath Bay
content:
The southern edge of the Jinxz Plains meets Witchbreath Bay, an unsettling body of water with a dire reputation. Strange fogs roll off the water without warning, thick and disorienting. Ships that anchor in the bay report disturbing dreams—visions of drowned things, voices from beneath the waves.

Even the goblins avoid Witchbreath Bay. That's saying something—Scalawags will raid nearly anything, go nearly anywhere. But the bay feels wrong in a way that penetrates even goblin recklessness. Broods that camp too close to the water's edge report members wandering off in the night, walking into the fog, never returning.

Elders in surrounding regions tell stories of something sleeping in the deep water, something that dreams, something that shouldn't be awakened. Whether this is truth or legend, the bay remains avoided by anyone with sense.

name: Witvein
content:
The eastern Nysanna Range, on its northern slopes facing the polar wastes, is dominated by the region known as Witvein—a maze of tunnel-laden glaciers that serves as home to the Wydlings, the ice goblins of the far north.

The Wydlings have carved an entire civilization into these glaciers, creating networks of passages, chambers, and hunting blinds that only they can navigate safely. The ice shifts constantly, and the Wydlings have learned to read its movements—knowing which tunnels will collapse within days and which will last generations. Outsiders who enter Witvein without Wydling guides rarely emerge.

Unlike their southern goblin cousins who live in massive communal broods, Wydlings move in small extended-family hunting packs. They are patient, methodical predators—capable of waiting motionless in snowdrifts for hours before striking. Their pale blue skin and razor-sharp icicle teeth mark them as creatures perfectly adapted to this frozen hell.

The Wydlings maintain an uneasy awareness of Niquous, the white dragon whose territory borders their own. They have lost enough hunters to him that they've developed an elaborate system of scouts, signals, and escape routes. When Niquous hunts, the Wydlings vanish deeper into their glacier tunnels, waiting in absolute stillness until the danger passes.

name: Wodhuubo
content: A city-state in Dalizi.

name: Woods of Systoril
content:
Tags: forest, geography

A dense, dark coastal forest east of Melodia and south of the Piktiniti Desert. The Woods of Systoril have a reputation for strangeness that keeps most travelers away—and those who enter often report experiences they struggle to explain.

name: Wurm Maw Bay
content:
Tags: bay, geography

TODO

name: Wykakas
content:
Tags: city, state

The southernmost Kor city-state in Atriik, built on the Sha Sonisst as the first line of defense against Naruaghin incursions from the Bloodlands.

See [Atriik](Atriik.md#wykakas) for full details.

name: Wyrm Teeth
content:
Tags: hills, geography

A range of hills in Westrim, east of Kura and north of the Milras Forest. The Wyrm Teeth earned their name from their jagged, tooth-like peaks and their historical connection to the wyrms that once roamed these lands.

name: Xcraya Pallir
content:
Tags: region, geography

A region in Malo Kon Greikh.

TODO

name: Xcraya River Valley
content:
Tags: river, valley, geography

A river valley in Malo Kon Greikh.

TODO

name: Xeltri Plateau
content:
Tags: plateau, giants, ogres, geography

A broad, elevated tableland in the northern Giant Lands, positioned between the Hills of Gezzeri to the north and Malo Kon Greikh to the south. The plateau was once home to a sophisticated ogre civilization—fortress cities built from the plateau's abundant stone, connected by paved roads and defended by disciplined ogre legions.

That civilization is gone. A few years ago, a massive warband of hill giants from Triuci swept across the plateau, demolishing everything in their path. The ogre city-states fell one by one, their inhabitants killed or scattered. The giants didn't stay to occupy—they simply destroyed and moved on, leaving the plateau a graveyard of shattered fortifications.

name: Xhodon
content:
Tags: city, state, nation

A city-state in Malo Kon Greikh.

TODO

name: Xi Chin
content:
The Timekeeper city rises from a high plateau in the central Tethryns, its marble architecture stark white against the dark volcanic stone. See the full state entry for details on its astronomical traditions, the Great Clocktower, and the Chroniclers' Compact that guarantees its neutrality.

From a distance, Xi Chin appears to glow at dawn and dusk—the marble catching the light when the surrounding peaks have fallen into shadow. Pilgrims, students, and diplomats make the climb along the Wister Road, a switchback path carved into the mountainside over centuries. The journey takes three days from the coast, or two if you're young and foolish enough to push through the night.

name: Xibli Islands
content:
Tags: archipelago, geography

A scattered archipelago east of the Aikon Tidal Islands, where the inhabitants have built their entire culture around competitive gift-giving. The Xibli don't trade—they give, and the giving creates debts that shape every relationship.

name: Yegnoth
content:
Tags: poi, landmark, megadungeon

A vertical shaft forty miles across, punched clean through the Myjornis Mountains into the earth below. The walls are smooth as glass—no ledges, no handholds, no erosion despite millennia of rain. Standing at the rim, the far side is lost in haze. The bottom has never been seen, though estimates based on dropped objects (and their failure to produce audible impact) suggest a depth of at least fifteen miles.

name: Yibiye
content:
Tags: city, state, nation, free-isles

The warded city, where ancient magic guards the harbor and old grudges run deeper than the sea. Yibiye is the northernmost of the Free Isles city-states, positioned near the Massacre Hills—a name that tells its own story. The Torvachi family has held this city for centuries, and they remember every slight, every betrayal, every drop of blood that bought their walls.

name: Yokani Cliffs
content:
Tags: cliffs, geography

Cliffs within Shyona.

TODO

name: Yugurbas Dyos
content:
Tags: geography, swamp, contested

The bloody ground between Hedroscobb and Glivornax—a transitional marsh where the southern Wildwood gives way to the Tolkarsus watershed. The Hedroscobbi call it the Algae Fields. The Glivornaxi call it Sacred Waters. Both call it theirs, and both have been dying over it for a century.

name: Yuki
content:
Tags: citystate, ve, frontier, goblin

A fortified city-state in the Suki Hills, standing as the first line of defense against Krell expansion into Chimean territory. Yuki is a city of watchers, trappers, and survivors—people who've built their lives around the knowledge that the swarm could come any day.

name: Yumasa
content: A city-state in Dalizi.

name: Zeror Gokh
content:
Tags: city, state, nation

A city-state in Malo Kon Greikh.

TODO

name: Zhorheim Mountains
content: Home to Zorheim, the dragon guardian of the Einwissel dwarves who traveled back from Celestia. The dragon can travel to Celestia easily as well. The dwarves know its true name, and vice versa—a foundation of enormous trust. Located in a mountain range forming part of the southwest side of the northlands, northeast of Ichneum, marking the northern edge of the forest region.

name: Zor
content:
Tags: city, state, nation, wycendeula, eyendra

Zor is the only permanent settlement in the Eyendra forest—a city-state of roughly 15,000 souls perched on the western shore of Moornik Bay, facing outward toward the sea and deliberately turning its back on the forest that surrounds it on three sides.

The city exists because a dragon permits it. Eight centuries of survival have taught Zor's people that some bargains, however strange, are worth keeping.

name: Zord Oshbag
content:
Tags: city, state, nation

A city-state in Malo Kon Greikh.

TODO

name: Zorheim Mountains
content:
Tags: mountains, geography

TODO

name: Zremmad Khag
content:
Tags: city, ruin

A ruined city-state on the Xeltri Plateau, destroyed by hill giants.

TODO

name: Abaddan
content:
A mining settlement in the central mountains, smaller than Phorysia but significant for its iron deposits. The Abaddan mines produce the base metal that Hephakean smiths transform into tools, weapons, and the infrastructure of the kingdom.

The town is rough, practical, and perpetually covered in ore dust. Workers cycle through on multi-year contracts, earn their pay, and leave. Few stay longer than they must—Abaddan lacks Phorysia's amenities or Agora's opportunities. It is a place for work, not life.

name: Adal
content: The second city, on the smaller Iri peak. More focused on practical applications of magic—forges, workshops, and the production facilities where university artifacts are manufactured for export.

name: Ageito
content:
The eastern anchor. Ageito sits where the Torga River meets the sea, a newer city that has grown rapidly as Gorath pushes toward Nashua. The docks here handle military supplies for the eastern front; the barracks house troops rotating to and from the fighting.

Ageito has a frontier energy that older Gorathi cities lack—fortunes being made and lost, opportunities for the ambitious, and a willingness to overlook questions about one's past. It's also the most diverse city in Gorath, with significant populations of assimilated peoples from the empire's various conquests.

name: Aglip Scha
content:
Tags: ruins, plague, pilgrimage, religion

A village abandoned during a plague called the Weeping Rot, now a pilgrimage site for death-cultists, plague-doctors, and those who seek to understand mortality. The dead remain where they fell, and the faithful come to sit among them.

name: Agony Stones
content:
Tags: geography, cursed, landmark, danger

The Agony Stones are not ruins. They are not a monument. They are not the remnants of some ancient civilization. They are the still-dying body of something that has been dying for millennia.

Spanning roughly forty miles east to west and twenty miles north to south, the Agony Stones dominate the southern reaches of Tythikerys. The formations are massive—individual "stones" can reach a mile across—and take the shape of strange, contorted, branching tubes, like the circulatory system of something impossibly vast, frozen mid-pulse and turned to rock.

name: Agora
content:
The third great city of Hephake, positioned in the western reaches of the kingdom along the trade routes to Camaran and Adron. Where Phorysia mines and Erebinthias guards, Agora trades. The city's markets handle goods flowing between the mountain kingdoms and the lowlands—dwarven metalwork heading down, grain and textiles heading up.

Agora's governor holds equal rank with Phorysia's, appointed directly by the king for acts of exceptional service. The current governor, Thorndin Silvervein, earned his position through a century of diplomatic work establishing trade agreements with the lowland nations. He is old, shrewd, and far more interested in profit than politics.

name: Al Khamilah
content: The largest of the Sal'emri settlements, a proper town of perhaps five thousand. Al Khamilah maintains a Tarkhon-style covered market, mosque-like temples to Tarkhon's fire-gods, and architecture that would not look out of place in Nektuna. The town serves as the informal capital of the Tarkhon enclave zone, positioned where the largest river meets the bay.

name: Al Muqlib
content: Upriver from Al Khamilah, Al Muqlib sits where the river narrows enough to bridge. The town controls the only reliable crossing point for miles, collecting tolls from caravans that might otherwise bypass the coastal settlements entirely. A small but prosperous place, fiercely independent.

name: Alegheim
content:
The forest covering northern Moigos, stretching from the Qulyintes River to the coast near Blink Creek. Alegheim is dense, wet, and dark — the canopy blocks most sunlight, and the ground is perpetually soggy. The trees are adapted to standing water, their roots spreading wide and shallow.

The Kappa consider Alegheim their natural territory. The swampy forest suits their physiology, and they've maintained hidden places throughout — meeting spots, caches, bolt-holes for when affairs in town become complicated. Humans and other races live in Alegheim too, in villages along the rivers and clearings, but they live with the understanding that the forest belongs to others.

The forest's edge approaches the Isle of the Night and the Gloomterror's influence. The western reaches of Alegheim are darker than they should be, and the things that live there are stranger than ordinary swamp creatures.

name: Alejendrina
content: A town in the central region, known for its pottery. The varied clays of the Bolsaña P'Suelos forest provide raw materials; local artisans have developed distinctive glazing techniques. Alejendrina ceramics are minor exports, nothing compared to Sondoros wine, but they have their admirers.

name: Alezre
content: A northern settlement in the foothills, near the approaches to Smokestack and the New Chimea border.

name: Alyzon
content: A smaller island settlement that's developed a reputation for training household slaves. Alyzon's graduates are prized for their obedience, skills, and presentation. Wealthy buyers specifically request Alyzon-trained servants.

name: Am Batr
content: A smaller port town on the bay's northern shore, Am Batr handles the fishing trade and serves as a waypoint for coastal traffic moving between the Tarns and the main Mueran ports. The town maintains a small shipyard that repairs rather than builds—good enough for fishing boats and coastal traders.

name: Amaro
content: A northern city near the Torulius Mountains, serving as the primary market for giant herder goods during migration seasons. For three months of the year, Amaro transforms into one of the largest trade fairs in Central Aboyinzu. The rest of the year, it's a quiet mining town wondering where everyone went.

name: Andestro River
content:
The Andestro winds through the southern reaches of the peninsula, fed by streams from the Phalkrene Mountains' lower slopes. Warmer and slower than the Vainye, it meanders through marshland before reaching the coast near Deadwater Bay.

The river is navigable but eerie. As it approaches Deadwater Bay, the wildlife along its banks grows sparse, then absent. Fish become scarce. Birds stop calling. The last few miles flow in absolute silence, the Andestro's waters growing clearer and more lifeless as they near their destination. Locals call this stretch the "Dying Mile," and avoid it.

name: Andvik
content:
South of Levke, Andvik guards the approaches from Nashua. This fortress-city has seen more combat than any other Strømgodden settlement, its walls scarred by generations of skirmishes with Ix'Vasyla raiders.

Andvik's warriors specialize in anti-lizardfolk tactics—fighting in the mountain passes where the cold-blooded Ix'Vasyla are at disadvantage, striking at dawn when the lizardfolk are sluggish, and maintaining the vigilance that has kept Nashua's forces from ever reaching Levke.

The city's **Venom-Scarred** regiment consists entirely of warriors who have survived Ix'Vasyla bites—a badge of honor that marks them as tested against Trømgar's most persistent enemy.

name: Aphoryis
content:
At the heart of Slumber Deep, where the stasis is absolute and nothing should be able to move, a fortress stands.

Aphoryis was not built so much as assembled. Its walls are ships, stacked and fused into impossible configurations. Its towers are the masts of a hundred vessels, reinforced with bone and coral and materials that resist identification. Its foundations rest on the preserved bodies of leviathans, their flesh hardened by centuries of pressure into something stronger than stone.

The fortress exists because its master found a way to move within the stasis—or perhaps because he predates it, and the stasis formed around him. Mirsalyenar the Cruel has occupied Aphoryis for longer than any surface records indicate. He may have been here when the leylines first intersected. He may have caused that intersection.

Within Aphoryis, the stasis does not apply. Servants move through its corridors—drowned sailors raised as mindless workers, captured Kendor forced into service, creatures from the deep that Mirsalyenar has bound to his will. The fortress maintains itself, grows, reaches outward into the suspended graveyard to harvest new materials from the endless supply of the lost.

The lich's phylactery rests somewhere in the fortress's deepest chamber. It cannot be destroyed there. Nothing can be destroyed in the absolute stasis zone—not by fire, not by force, not by the most powerful magic ever devised. Mirsalyenar chose this location specifically because it renders him truly immortal. Even if his body is destroyed on the surface, his phylactery will regenerate him in a place no enemy can reach.

name: Apuindi Mountains
content:
Tags: mountains, geography

Mountains on Pesalolo.

TODO

name: Argum Hills
content:
Tags: hills, geography

A hill range in the Wanderlands.

TODO

name: Asgrael (Village)
content:
Population: ~250

A village along the Sova Chyda's middle reaches, within sight of the Winterwood's edge. Asgrael serves pilgrims traveling to the sacred forest and foresters who harvest timber from the woodland's outer edges. (Unlike Klevnaf's total prohibition on logging, Istora permits sustainable harvesting from Winterwood's periphery—a theological difference that both sides consider evidence of the other's corruption.)

name: Aspenthar (Town)
content:
Population: ~900

A logging town along the Rabbit River's middle reaches. Aspenthar processes timber from the surrounding forest and ships it downstream to Nøsen. The town's sawmills run constantly during the brief summer months, stockpiling lumber against the long winter.

name: Aster
content:
Tags: city, state, nation

A city-state within Griselia.

TODO

name: Astrella Forest
content:
The beautiful forest. Ancient trees with silver-gray bark. Clearings filled with flowers that bloom in moonlight. The Gyv consider Astrella sacred—or as sacred as a culture of manipulators can consider anything.

Important rituals happen here. Coming-of-age ceremonies. Marriages between powerful families. The creation of Tested Bonds between Gyv who want genuine relationships. Astrella is one of the few places where Gyv try to be honest with each other.

Try.

name: Avar Sonnon
content: Eastern city, near the Crags of Geth and Griselia. Deals with occasional goblin incursions (theatrical or otherwise).

name: Avil Lakes
content:
A cluster of shallow lakes in the southern plains, fed by seasonal streams that flow down from the desert highlands before the land dries completely. The lakes expand in the wet season and shrink to muddy pools in the dry, but they never vanish entirely.

Avil Lakes mark the southern extent of reliable water—and therefore the southern extent of mammoth territory. The herds come here during migrations, and the Silzar have learned to read the lakes' water levels as omens for the season ahead. High water means good grazing; low water means hard times coming.

The lakes also serve as a neutral meeting point with the desert traders who bring metal up from the south. Neither side trusts the other enough to enter their territory, so they meet at Avil Lakes, conduct business quickly, and part ways.

name: Avuul (Village)
content:
Population: ~180

A highland village in the Suftos Hills, positioned to watch the eastern approaches. Avuul has been evacuated and resettled three times during the civil war as the front lines shifted. The current residents are mostly soldiers and their families—civilians who can handle themselves in a fight.

name: Ay'Nbhad
content:
Tags: poi

Like burning man, but is located on the River Yuforhi, which cuases ephoria when drunk. People from across the world gather to party, share music, dance, fuck, and have fun. Also, giant pain orgies, where everyone hurts each other for the pleasure.

Very sketchy, as it's located in the White Wastes which are filled with roaming goblin and orcish tribes.

name: Azur
content: The largest village, positioned along the primary path to the monastery. Pilgrims and the rare traders stop here before the final ascent. The residents are laypeople—families who support the monastery through farming and crafts but haven't taken full vows. Life is simple, governed by the monastery's rhythms.

name: Banguna
content:
A small village east of Moura, in a clearing near the jungle's edge. Banguna is where the **harvesters** live—the workers who cultivate, collect, and process the jungle's psychedelic bounty.

**The Cultivation Fields**: The villagers maintain controlled growing areas where specific pod varieties are encouraged. Different strains produce different effects; the sacramental preparation used in the Pool ritual requires precise combinations.

**The Processing Houses**: Raw pod-sap must be refined before use. The Processing Houses are workshops where this happens—drying, filtering, combining, aging. The work is skilled and dangerous. Errors can produce preparations that kill, or worse.

**The Workers**: Banguna's population is mixed: some Velwey, many humans and elves hired for their steady hands and expendable minds. The workers here are constantly exposed to psychoactive residue. They see things others don't. They're not always sure what's real. Turnover is high.

Population: ~1,200.

name: Barren Hills
content:
Tags: hills, geography

Hills in the Dragon's Spine Coast.

TODO

name: Barro Triste
content:
A village in the eastern region whose name—"sad mud"—reflects its primary characteristic. The ground here is perpetually damp, the weather perpetually overcast, and the mood perpetually melancholic. The village exists because someone has to farm the rich bottomland soils, and the crops grown in sad mud are nutritious if joyless.

Barro Triste has produced several notable Amverela poets, all writing extensively about rain, grey skies, and the weight of existence. Literary scholars consider this a coincidence. No one else does.

name: Bathemiel
content:
The dragon came to these islands in the Second Eon, when the world was younger and the Free Isles were uninhabited rock. No one knows why Bathemiel chose to stay, or what wounded it so badly that it needed to sleep. When humans arrived, they found the dragon already chained—great bonds of dull gray metal that resist all attempts at identification, anchored to the cavern walls by mechanisms no one has been able to understand.

The first Gattorini patrón made a deal. The exact terms are lost, recorded only in a sealed vault that subsequent patróns have been unable (or unwilling) to open. What remains is the gift: the ruling patrón of Mpehi can transform any non-living matter they touch into pure gold. The effect is permanent and irreversible.

The gift has made the Gattorini unimaginably wealthy—but it has costs. Every patrón eventually goes mad, convinced that gold is spreading through their own flesh. Several have died clawing at their skin, certain they were transforming. Others have become paranoid about touch, refusing to remove their gloves even in private. The current patrón, **Donna Serephina Gattorini**, wears golden gauntlets at all times and has not touched another living being in fifteen years.

Bathemiel occasionally speaks through dreams. The messages are cryptic, unsettling, and never helpful. The dragon seems to be waiting for something. The Gattorini have learned not to ask what.

name: Bazaar of Scales
content:
Tags: poi, settlement, trade

A sprawling desert market in the southern Luquihn Desert, positioned at the edge of the Lyagnadarr Mountains. The Bazaar of Scales is one of the few places where surface-dwellers can reliably trade with the Murazi—the underground merfolk who dwell beneath the desert.

The bazaar takes its name from its primary trade goods: scales, hides, and bones from desert creatures, along with the iridescent scales the Murazi harvest from subterranean fish. The market operates in a shallow canyon where ancient wells tap into underground water sources—likely Murazi engineering, though they neither confirm nor deny it.

Trade here follows its own rules. The Murazi emerge at dawn and dusk when temperatures are tolerable, conducting business through intermediaries who have learned their trade-sign language. Violence in the bazaar is met with swift collective punishment—the Murazi simply seal the wells, and without water, the surface market dies. This threat keeps even the most unscrupulous merchants civil.

The bazaar also serves as a waypoint for travelers heading to or from the Lyagnadarr Mountains, and a place to hire guides who know the deep desert routes.

name: Bazz
content:
The de facto capital of modern Mueras, built on a series of cliff-carved terraces overlooking the junction of Labyrinth Sound and Corpse Bay. Where Kaspion concentrated power in a single ruler, Bazz concentrates it in the Council—the assembly of merchant-kings who theoretically govern collectively.

The city holds perhaps forty thousand people, making it the largest settlement in Mueras by a significant margin. The waterfront terraces house the trading companies, warehouses, and pilot-guild headquarters. The middle terraces contain markets, craftsmen, and the homes of successful merchants. The upper terraces—carved deepest into the cliff—hold the estates of the merchant-king families and the Council chambers.

Bazz's architecture is vertical rather than horizontal. Streets are often stairs. Goods move by crane and pulley as often as by cart. The wealthy live highest, where sea-spray doesn't reach and the view encompasses the entire Sound. The poor cluster at water level, where flooding is regular and the damp never quite leaves the walls.

name: Beauty Bay
content:
South of Tyberoskos, where the Maun River meets the sea, Beauty Bay earned its name honestly. Something about the water's mineral content—runoff from the limestone sinkholes upstream—combined with the angle of the Pearly Mountains creates sunsets of extraordinary color. Oranges and purples that seem impossible, light that paints the water like oil on canvas.

Artists from Maun have painted Beauty Bay for centuries. Some claim watching the sunset from its shores inspires creativity; others say that's romantic nonsense, but they keep coming back anyway. The bay has become part of Maun's identity—the School of Music holds outdoor performances in a natural stone amphitheater on the eastern shore, timing finales to coincide with the sunset's peak.

The same minerals that color the light make the bay's water unsuitable for drinking but excellent for certain dye-making processes. Maun's theatrical costumes are famous partly for colors that can't be reproduced elsewhere—the bay's gift to the city that celebrates it.

name: Bejakørn (Town)
content:
Population: ~600

A highland town in the eastern reaches, positioned where the Sova Chyda descends from its mountain sources. Bejakørn serves as Istora's gateway to the Dragon's Spine Coast—limited trade still flows through here, and the town's inns host travelers heading east or west.

The town also houses Istora's armories and training grounds, positioned far from the front lines where they can operate without interruption. Young elves from across the kingdom come here to learn the warrior's arts before deployment to the Suftos line.

name: Belén
content:
A southern city near the Skyloft Castle ruins, drawing scholars and treasure-hunters interested in whatever caused the fortress to fall. Belén's economy has become dependent on these visitors—guides, supplies, lodging, and discreet medical care for those who return injured.

The city council officially discourages exploration of the ruins. The city economy would collapse without it.

name: Belu Jenari
content:
Tags: poi, volcano

A massive volcanic mountain rising from the center of the Sandreach desert—a dramatic landmark visible for miles across the barren landscape. This volcano was the site where the legendary Demon Horn of Fire was forged, drawing on the convergence of fire and light leylines beneath the mountain.

name: Besnoumeru
content:
Besnoumeru is an extremely wealthy city within Catalina, where merchants have made fortunes by predicting changes in supply and demand with uncanny accuracy. Rent is impossibly expensive—very few options exist for those on a budget. The gorgeous city architecture reflects centuries of wealth accumulation, and its position on the river delta makes it a crucial trade hub.

However, Besnoumeru's prosperity comes with a dark edge: the city is secretly controlled by time-gangs, criminal organizations that use temporal manipulation to maintain their iron grip on commerce and politics.

name: Besnoumeru
content:
Nektuna's second city, built on a **time leyline**. The Oracle Merchants born here can sense market changes before they happen—not prophecy, but an instinct for timing that has made Besnoumeru almost as wealthy as Tarkhetan.

The city has an eerie calm. Nobody rushes. The merchants already know what's coming.

name: Bettle Lake
content:
A mountain lake in the northeastern Scarlet Peaks, one of the few permanent water sources in the range. Bettle Lake sits in a high valley accessible only through narrow passes, making it naturally defensible. A small community of herders lives near the lake, raising hardy mountain goats and trading wool and cheese with travelers.

The lake is unusually clear and cold, fed by underground springs rather than surface runoff. Local legend claims something lives in the depths—not hostile, but watchful. Herders report seeing large shapes moving beneath the surface on moonlit nights, though no one has ever been attacked.

name: Bhutag
content: A town in the eastern desert near the coast, built atop millennia-old catacombs. The locals avoid the deeper tunnels. Artifacts occasionally emerge from the depths, sold to collectors who don't ask questions about what else might be down there.
