Tags: poi, sarakiel, rakite

A marble fortress at the northern edge of Tyror Miig, where the plains give way to the Ves Kanis headwaters. Home to a community of fallen Sarakiel who've been here for a thousand years, slowly forgetting what they used to be.

The Palace sits at the intersection of the Light and Void leylines—where Kunus and Nilus cross. The original Sarakiel didn't choose this location by accident.

name: Palace of Light
content: A fortress-monastery built directly over the point where the light leyline surfaces, located in the high Widebarrow Mountains on the border between Dern and Thorum. The original structure dates to the late Second Eon, though it has been rebuilt, expanded, and partially destroyed multiple times across millennia.

name: Partsmouth
content: An interior settlement surrounded by what remains of the island's forests. Logging operations here supply timber for ships and construction, though the trees are thinning and no one invests in replanting. The workers are mostly transported criminals serving out sentences; the overseers are mostly locals who've concluded that guarding lumberjacks beats guarding prisoners.

name: Pastrova
content: A town famous for one of the best law schools in Alaria. Scholars come from across the Middle Sea to study here. Bestacian legal experts are in high demand—they've made careers out of finding loopholes and crafting airtight contracts.

name: Pathanul
content:
Tags: forest, geography, ve, avalon, time-magic

A forest of ancient trees spreading across northern Avalon, where the kingdom's heartland meets the Sestran border. The priests claim this is where time moves slowest in Avalon—and whether that's metaphor or reality, travelers consistently report disorientation in Pathanul's depths.

name: Pavik's Cave
content: A coastal cave system on the western shore, near the Gulf of Kazül. Local legends speak of something living in the depths.

name: Pebble Islands
content:
Tags: island, geography

A group of islands in Tarkhon.

TODO

name: Peecked Hills
content: Western hills between the coast and the desert interior.

name: Peleletra Hills
content:
Tags: hills, geography

Hills in the Farlands.

TODO

name: Pelera
content:
Tags: dragon, dark, the-glog

A dark emerald dragon who claims The Glog forest as her domain, laired at Shimmer Lake on the forest's eastern edge. Pelera rules through fear and mystery rather than overt destruction—every shadow in her territory might contain her, every silence might herald her approach.

She tolerates human activity at the margins but responds lethally to intrusions into her core territory. The forest's reputation for making travelers feel watched isn't superstition—it's Pelera.

For full details, see the dragon entry: [Pelera](../../bestiary/dragons/Pelera.md)

name: Pfustias
content:
Tags: dragon, creature

A dragon in Ogzwad Swamp.

TODO

name: Phiona Bay
content:
The western coast of SW Urok curves inward to form Phiona Bay, a broad shallow anchorage sheltered by the Pearly Mountains' western extent. The bay takes its name from Saint Phiona, a healer-priestess who, according to legend, walked across its waters during a great plague three centuries past. She touched the sick in every fishing village along the shore, and by morning the plague had broken. Whether miracle or myth, the bay is considered blessed water by many sailors.

Phiona Bay is treacherous for the unwary. Sandbars shift with the seasons, submerged rocks lurk where charts say clear water should be, and the currents run strange near the river mouths. Local pilots know the channels; outsiders founder. The fishing villages along the shore make good money guiding merchant ships to safe anchorage—and salvaging those that didn't pay for guidance.

The Vyandrik River empties into the bay's northern reach, wide and slow and navigable for shallow-draft vessels. The Prelapon joins it from the south, faster and prone to seasonal flooding. Together they drain most of the Pearly Mountains' western slopes, carrying silt that builds the sandbars and makes the bay's navigation so unpredictable.

On feast days commemorating Phiona's Walk, small boats with candle-lit shrines drift across the bay after sunset. Some say the candles burn for days without going out. Others say that's just good wax and wishful thinking.

name: Pholiki
content: Easternmost city-state, closest to Erasnus. Primary point of contact for trade and theatrical diplomacy. Pholiki's brood-father typically leads the delegations that travel to Traduci to make grandiose invasion threats before signing trade agreements.

name: Phorysia
content: The capital city of Hephake, a major dwarven mining settlement with massive silver extraction operations. The city sprawls across multiple mountain levels, its architecture dictated by the seams of silver that honeycomb the surrounding rock. Smoke from refineries hangs perpetually over the lower districts.

name: Piedritas
content:
A town near the Sondoros forest, existing primarily to support the wine industry. During harvest season, Piedritas swells with workers; the rest of the year, it maintains equipment, ages wine in vast cellars, and waits.

The town has developed its own culture, distinct from coastal Qintas—rougher, more practical, defined by the annual rhythm of the harvest. Vineyard workers from across the region gather here, sharing techniques and gossip. Some are human; the Amverela prefer to employ outsiders for the physical labor.

name: Pino River
content:
The Pino drains the southwestern Wilds, smaller and slower than the Byzkus. It flows through territory the Dwelyn nominally claim but have never actually occupied—too far from Lise, too close to Naruaghin raiding routes. The river eventually reaches the coast somewhere near the Gulf of Norag's contested waters.

No one lives along the Pino. Occasionally a Dwelyn hunting party will follow it for a few days before turning back. The forest here is quieter than it should be—fewer birds, fewer insects, fewer of the usual jungle sounds. Hunters call it "dead water" and avoid it when they can.

name: Plainwatch
content:
The frontier outpost, built on a ridge where the mountains give way to the plains. Plainwatch is less a city than a fortified observation post with a permanent garrison. Its towers provide sight lines across dozens of miles of satyr territory.

Plainwatch exists to provide early warning. If the satyr ever unite—if the rumored king consolidates power—the signal fires from Plainwatch will give Graystone and Ironforge time to seal their defenses. The garrison rotates frequently; posting to Plainwatch is considered both honorable and deeply tedious.

The city has perhaps 2,000 permanent residents, mostly military families and support personnel. Civilian population fluctuates with threat assessment.

name: Pool to Infinity
content:
Tags: lake, geography

A lake in the Dalizi Wildlands.

TODO

name: Porcuni
content:
Tags: forest, geography

A forest within Illron.

TODO

name: Port Echo
content:
The largest settlement on the Beak, named for the acoustic phenomenon in its harbor. The surrounding cliffs create a natural amphitheater that amplifies and echoes sounds from the water—a whispered conversation in a boat can sometimes be heard clearly on the docks. This makes Port Echo useless for clandestine meetings but excellent for public announcements.

Port Echo serves as the primary embarkation point for North Agreben and the Greenwater Isles beyond. Ferries run regularly, weather permitting, and the town has developed a modest shipbuilding industry using timber floated down from the Taflex Forest.

name: Port Edott
content: A western harbor town, primary departure point for ships heading to the mainland. More traffic passes through here than the town's size would suggest. Sylke maintains a garrison to monitor shipping; the locals resent it but comply.

name: Port Perith
content:
The last "normal" port before the haunted zone—normal being relative in Mueras. Port Perith exists because someone has to service the ships that trade with the cursed coast, and the sailors who work those routes need somewhere to drink that isn't actively haunted.

The town is tense, well-armed, and superstitious. Every building has iron fixtures at the doors and windows. Salt lines are refreshed daily. The local temple to the death-gods does excellent business. Port Perith's residents are not friendly to outsiders, but they're professional—if you have coin, they'll trade.

name: Prince's Bay
content:
The large southern bay where Enavadi's coast curves toward Gyosha's waters. The name carries a double meaning that locals argue about endlessly.

The historical explanation: centuries ago, a prince of Veldoraine wrecked his ship in this bay during a storm. The Enavadi rescued him, healed his injuries, and then—true to their isolationist traditions—refused to let him leave. Once you've lived among them, once you've seen how they fish, you don't go back to tell outsiders. Prince Aldric of Veldoraine lived out his decades as a fisherman, reportedly became quite skilled at it, and is buried somewhere along the bay's northern shore. His descendants, if any survive, have been Enavadi for generations.

The practical explanation: "prince" is Enavadi slang for an exceptionally large catch. Prince's Bay is where the biggest fish school, so it's "where you catch a prince."

Ask an Enavadi fisherman which explanation is correct, and they'll smile and say "both."

Somewhere in Veldoraine's dusty archives, there's still a theoretical claim to "Prince Aldric of the Enavadi Shore." No one has pressed it in two hundred years, and any envoy who tried would find the Enavadi remarkably unimpressed by foreign titles.

name: Prisoner Hills
content:
Tags: hills, geography

Hills in Malo Kon Greikh.

TODO

name: Promise of Mythras
content:
A massive stone object, permanently trapping the Sennites within Ennros Lake. Named after Mythras, the great dwarven family and king of Argysis before it fell to the dragon. When they got revenge, they placed a powerful enchantment on lake, barring the Sennites from ever leaving the lake. The Sennites would pay very handsomely for its removal.

On it is engraved, "A promise, once broken, can be no better reforged than a sword of diamond".

If a sword of diamond is brought to the site and broken, the enchantment is lifted.

Diamonds necessary to make an entire sword can only be found deep underground (verrrry deep) where it would be quite sketchy to go.

name: Providence
content:
Tags: forest, geography

A forest within Shyona.

TODO

name: Prowling Hills
content:
Tags: hills, geography

Hills within the Crystal Mountains.

TODO

name: Puerto Carmele
content:
A coastal town south of Mataojo, smaller and quieter. Puerto Carmele was once a significant port; silting and shifting currents have reduced its harbor to shallow-draft vessels only. The town persists on fishing and nostalgia.

Some Amverela families maintain estates here specifically because it's unfashionable—a retreat from the sophistication of Constancias and the commerce of Mataojo. The pace of life in Puerto Carmele is slow even by high elf standards.

name: Pugrai D'had
content:
The northernmost lake, where the Krelfassa river meets the coast. D'had is the shallowest of the three and the stillwater effect is relatively mild—boats can cross with effort. The river mouth creates a mixing zone where normal water meets stillwater, producing strange currents that curl back on themselves.

Local legend claims D'had was once a harbor in ancient times, before the leylines shifted. No archaeological evidence supports this, though the lakebed is littered with debris too deep to recover easily.

name: Pugrai Naihim
content:
The largest and most dangerous lake, fed by the Elmynfrosk river from the north. Naihim's center is effectively impassable—the stillwater effect is so pronounced that even objects thrown in arc slowly rather than splash. Local satyr tribes consider the lake sacred or cursed (the distinction is unclear) and avoid its shores entirely.

What makes Naihim unique is the sound. On still nights, those near the shore report a low, constant vibration—not quite a hum, more a pressure in the ears. The sound comes from the water itself, as if something vast is holding very, very still at the bottom.

name: Pugrai Shisk
content:
The smallest lake, positioned between D'had and Naihim. Shisk is notable for its clarity—the stillwater effect means sediment never rises, and visibility extends to the bottom in most areas. Fish exist here but move in slow, languid patterns. They're easy to catch but taste faintly metallic.

Rumors persist of shapes visible in Shisk's depths—regular geometric forms that don't match natural formations. No one has successfully dived deep enough to investigate; the water's resistance makes descent exhausting and ascent terrifying.

name: Pul
content: A smaller settlement higher up the mountain, home to monks in the middle stages of their training. The buildings here are even more austere than Azur's—stone boxes with minimal furnishings, designed to minimize distraction from practice.

name: Puma Wood
content:
The forest that contains and defines Rilyn, spreading across the northeastern slopes of the Screech Peaks and down toward the Fernius Sound coast. Puma Wood is old-growth forest — massive trees that have stood for centuries, their canopy creating a green twilight below. The Splinkreen have lived here since their transformation, building their city among the trees rather than clearing them.

The forest is named for the large cats that once hunted here, though the pumas are rarely seen now. Whether they've retreated deeper into the woods or simply grown more cautious around the Splinkreen is unclear. The name persists regardless.

name: Pyndar
content: A port town that handles legitimate trade—the non-slave commerce that Tykos needs to survive. Grain imports, luxury goods, weapons, and other necessities flow through Pyndar. The merchants here maintain relationships with traders who won't deal in Slavos's markets.

name: Pyoa Noi
content:
Within the eastern Nysanna Range stands one of the most bizarre landmarks in the Northlands: Pyoa Noi, a hundred-mile-long stone serpent that twists through the mountains, its petrified body diving in and out of the peaks like a sea serpent frozen mid-leap.

The serpent was once a living creature of unimaginable size, turned to stone by an incredibly powerful gorgon in an ancient battle. Over centuries, the Wydlings have hollowed out the serpent's interior, creating a network of tunnels and chambers within the stone body. Pyoa Noi is their greatest stronghold—a place where the ice cannot shift and the dragons cannot easily reach them.

The Wydlings defend Pyoa Noi fiercely from those who would mine it further. The stone of the petrified serpent has unusual properties that outside interests would love to exploit, but the Wydlings consider it sacred—the belly of a dead god, in their telling, and the only permanent home their people have ever known.

name: Pythalomos
content:
Tags: dragon, creature

A dragon of Westrim.

TODO

name: Queen Haegstra's Tower
content:
A ruined fortress in the northwestern interior of Phyr Island, deep within the Jungle of Spines. The tower was Haegstra's seat of power during her forty-three-year reign, and the site from which she worked the curse that transformed the island.

The tower still stands, though barely. The upper floors have collapsed. The walls are cracked and overgrown with crystalline vines. The main gate hangs open, has hung open for three centuries, as if inviting visitors.

No one goes there.

The Jungle of Spines is at its worst near the tower—the spines longer, sharper, the curse stronger. Creatures that would flee humans elsewhere on the island grow aggressive near the tower, as if defending it. The few expeditions that have attempted to reach the ruins report feeling watched, hearing sounds that couldn't be natural, seeing movement in the peripheral vision that vanished when looked at directly.

Some expeditions have reached the tower. Fewer have returned. Those who did describe:
- Chambers filled with statues that weren't soldiers—islanders, Haegstra's subjects, arranged in poses of supplication or service
- A throne room with a throne of fused stone, multiple petrified bodies melted together into a seat
- Sounds from below, from chambers beneath the tower, a rhythmic pulse like breathing or grinding stone
- The certainty, irrational but absolute, that something down there was aware of them

**The Petros Shard**: Beneath the tower, in chambers carved long before Haegstra arrived, lies the source of her power: a fragment of elemental earth roughly the size of a human torso, pulsing with petrification magic. The shard is not truly alive, but it responds to proximity and intent. It grants petrification abilities to those who bond with it—touch it, accept its presence in their mind, let it partially transform their flesh.

Haegstra found the shard when she first came to Phyr. She was its third known wielder. The ruins beneath the tower predate human settlement on Ve; whatever species carved them left no other trace.

The shard is why the curse persists. It's the engine that powers everything—the spine sickness, the warped creatures, the petrified army. Destroying it might end the curse. Or it might release three centuries of accumulated magical energy in a single catastrophic burst.

**What Haegstra Became**: When Haegstra petrified the army, the magical backlash didn't kill her. It *dispersed* her.

Her consciousness fused with the curse itself. She didn't die—she became the crystalline spines, the petrified reefs, the stone soldiers, the warped creatures. The curse IS Haegstra. Every spine that breaks skin carries a fragment of her awareness. Every statue contains a sliver of her attention. The island is her body now.

For three centuries, she's been fragmented—present everywhere but coherent nowhere, her mind shattered across thousands of cursed objects. But she's slowly reassembling. The shared dreams that occasionally sweep through Attla are Haegstra becoming focused enough to reach out intentionally. The lights in the tower are her attention gathering at the shard, the center of her distributed self.

The Attla elders understand this. They've tracked the dreams for generations, noting their increasing frequency and clarity. Thirty years ago, the dreams came once a decade. Now they come several times a year. Haegstra is waking up.

She won't have a body. But within another generation, she'll likely be coherent enough to direct the curse precisely—extend spines toward specific targets, control the creatures as extensions of her will, perhaps animate the stone soldiers as puppets. The island will stop being passively dangerous and become actively hostile.

The elders don't share this knowledge with outsiders. They don't want treasure hunters getting ideas about "killing the curse" by destroying the shard. An uncontrolled release could petrify everything within miles—including Attla.

name: Quiet Mountain
content:
Tags: mountain, geography

See entry under Krenglelan Range.

name: Rabbit River
content: Tangiern's primary trade artery. Rising in the southern Fragenstor Mielthøn, it flows south through the kingdom's heartland past Nøsen and into the Void. Named for the abundance of snowshoe hares along its banks, though locals claim the name actually references how quickly it floods—here one moment, everywhere the next.

name: Recnaf
content:
On the southern shore of the eastern peninsula, Recnaf is Roule's quietest city—a sheltered harbor town more focused on fishing than politics. The city lacks Fozyader's grandeur, Teldib's military importance, or Kabilcuz's historical pretensions. Locals consider this a virtue.

Recnaf's fishing fleet is the largest in Roule, and the city's markets supply much of the nation's seafood. The work is hard, the people are practical, and outsiders who put on airs are quietly mocked. There's a stubbornness here that the other cities lack—Recnaf was settled by colonists who wanted distance from halfling high society, and that independence persists.

The city maintains informal trade relationships with communities the Honey Lords would prefer not to acknowledge. Fishing boats sometimes return with cargo that didn't come from the sea. The city watch asks few questions. Recnaf takes care of its own.

name: Redreed Lake
content:
Tags: lake, geography

Redreed Lake is a terminal lake in southern Tythikerys, fed by the Dutunimako River and ringed by distinctive reddish reeds. It serves as one of the few permanent water sources in the southern reaches of orc territory—and it sits uncomfortably close to the Agony Stones.

name: Ribwood Forest
content:
A forest on the southern Tyvern Islands, home to a rare and valuable tree species known as ribwood. This remarkable wood is completely fireproof — it doesn't burn under any circumstances, making it incredibly valuable for shipbuilding. Wealthy merchants and pirates prize ribwood vessels above all others, as they're immune to fire arrows, Greek fire, and the countless other flame-based hazards of naval warfare.

However, harvesting ribwood comes at a cost. The Tuktuk people live in and around the Ribwood Forest, and they fiercely resent outsiders who ravage their homeland for profit. Logging expeditions into the forest frequently end in violence, with Tuktuk warriors — particularly those from Minos village — ambushing the loggers. Despite the danger, the lure of fireproof ships ensures that treasure-seekers continue to risk the Tuktuk's wrath to harvest the precious wood.

name: Riki River
content:
The easternmost river, closest to the Dalizi border and the School of Summoning. The Riki Valley is the most rural and the most Rentar-influenced—fewer human farms, more monasteries, and a growing atmosphere of religious intensity.

**Major Settlements:**
- **Prozny** (~5,000): The largest town in the Riki Valley, serving as a waystation for travelers to the School of Summoning.

name: Ripmaw Sound
content:
Tags: sea, geography, hazard

The western waters between Iqes and the Upoceax mainland, Ripmaw Sound earned its name from the volcanic rock formations that lurk beneath its surface. Ships that stray from the known channels find their hulls ripped open by jagged basalt ridges invisible until the moment of impact.

The sound is navigable — generations of Qindo sailors have mapped safe passages through its dangers — but the channels shift. Volcanic activity continues deep below, slowly raising new ridges and collapsing old ones. Charts more than a decade old are unreliable. Charts from outsiders are worthless.

name: River of Blades
content:
The easternmost river has earned its grim name honestly. It flows through the Glass Fields—a stretch of volcanic obsidian that the Sundering exposed and subsequent eruptions expanded. The riverbed is littered with natural glass shards ranging from pebble-sized splinters to blade-length fragments, all edges keen enough to slice leather.

No one boats the River of Blades. The glass would shred any hull within minutes. Fording is possible only at specific points where the Fengruk have laid down iron grates, and even then, a fall means cuts that take weeks to fully heal. The river runs red after storms, not from mineral content but from the blood of animals foolish enough to drink from its banks.

Despite—or because of—its dangers, the River of Blades attracts a specific clientele. Weaponsmiths prize volcanic obsidian for certain ritual blades, and the Glass Fields produce some of the finest natural specimens in Alaria. Collection expeditions from Durkarn make the journey several times a year, harvesting glass under the protection of heavily armored handlers. The obsidian trade is small but lucrative.

The river empties into the strait through a delta of black sand—pulverized glass worn down by the current. The delta settlement, if it can be called that, is a seasonal camp of obsidian traders and the few pilots willing to navigate the eastern strait channels. They call the place Blackmouth, and it has no permanent structures.

name: River Yuforhi
content:
River of euphoria-inducing substance, most intense during the hottest part of the year. Runs through the White Wastes, so impossible to have long term settlement. However, people will meet at Ay'Nbhad to celebrate for a week straight once every year.

The substance also makes pain feel good if you have enough of it, which can result in horrible deaths.

name: Rompur Liko
content: The site of the annual winter bazaar—a small island cliff connected to the surrounding canyon walls by rope bridges. Once a year, all tribes gather here to trade, celebrate, and renew bonds. Then they scatter back to their territories.

name: Roye (Town)
content:
The only other settlement large enough to call a town, Roye sits in the northeastern lowlands where several secondary roads converge. It serves as the market hub for the eastern villages and the coastal settlements, handling the fish trade that flows inland and the grain that flows out.

Roye is smaller than Nylen and less concerned with politics. The locals joke that Nylen talks while Roye works. The town has a reputation for practical folk who care more about getting things done than debating about it.

name: Ruby Rock
content:
West of the Deadwalk desert, within the Scorches, stands Ruby Rock—a massive stone outcropping that erupted from the ground in ancient times. The rock contains a vast cavern system filled with rubies and gemstones, created when mineral-rich water from the hotsprings deposited sediments over millennia before being uplifted by geological forces.

The gems are breathtakingly beautiful and incredibly valuable, but they carry a curse. Those who take rubies from the cavern find themselves plagued by misfortune—relationships crumble, fortunes are lost, and death stalks them. Some say the curse is tied to the volcanic forces that created the gems; others believe the rubies remember being torn from their home and seek revenge on those who stole them.

name: Ryerton & Bexley
content: Market towns in the agricultural heart of Tornia. The farmland here is rich, and these towns serve as collection points for grain and produce—now requisitioned by occupation authorities at "fair" prices that aren't.

name: Sakatia
content:
Tags: ruins, village, faewoods, old-tolaria

A ruined village of Old Tolaria, swallowed by the Faewoods when the forest erupted from nothing. Now half-consumed by impossible growth, it serves as a warning about what the Faewoods do to things that stay too long.

name: Salamander Cave
content:
Despite its name suggesting fire creatures, Salamander Cave in northern Scheyolonae is home to ice salamanders—pale, cold-adapted amphibians the size of large dogs that breathe frost rather than flame. The caves extend deep into the frozen earth, and the salamanders have carved out an extensive network of tunnels.

The ice salamanders are not aggressive unless provoked, but they fiercely defend their territory. Their frost breath can freeze exposed flesh in seconds, and they hunt in packs. Travelers who stumble into their caves rarely emerge.

name: Salenial
content:
Tags: village, ruins, contaminated

A ruined mining village in Eshbya Hills, within Deo Esari's territory. Abandoned 200 years ago during the southern contamination crisis.

name: Salt Tomb
content:
On the western shore of Deadman's Lake stands the Salt Tomb—a burial structure made entirely of salt blocks, gleaming white against the dark water. The tomb predates any local memory; no one knows who built it or who (or what) is buried inside. The salt has not dissolved despite centuries of exposure to weather, which should be impossible.

The tomb is sealed. The few who've tried to break in have found the salt harder than stone, and the one expedition that tried tunneling underneath reported their tools corroding to rust within hours. Whatever's in the Salt Tomb, it's staying there.

name: Samandoken
content:
The western port of Samandoken faces the Inside Passage and serves as Roule's administrative center for Nylos. Government officials, tax collectors, and military officers rotate through here before deployment to the subjugated islands. The city has a transient quality—people are always coming or going, and long-term residents are outnumbered by those passing through.

Samandoken is where Nylos resistance fighters are tried and sentenced. The courthouse is the city's largest building, deliberately imposing. Executions happen in the public square. The message to any Nylosi who might be watching is clear.

Despite its grim official function, Samandoken has a lively merchant quarter. Goods from Nylos—fish, pearls, worked coral—flow through here on their way to Roulean markets. The prices are set in Samandoken, and Nylosi sellers have no leverage to negotiate.

name: San Isidrio
content: A central city along the main road between Cañas and Qintas. San Isidrio has positioned itself as a waystation for travelers and merchants, offering comfortable inns, reliable supplies, and absolutely nothing memorable. This is intentional—the city profits from traffic passing through, not from giving anyone reason to linger.

name: Sandskull
content:
Tags: poi, ruin

A ruined fortress or temple at the southern extent of the Kunagi Hills, where the ridges flatten into the desert plain. The distinctive skull-shaped rock formation that gives it its name is visible for miles across the flat terrain.

Sandskull predates Atriik's founding by unknown centuries. The architecture shares some features with Urk Noulgas in the Wyrm Teeth—suggesting a connection to the ancient wyrm-keepers who once controlled the giant sand wurms of the Kunagi. However, no direct link has been established, and the ruins' true purpose remains unknown.

name: Sandstorm Straits
content:
Tags: strait, geography

A turbulent stretch of water separating the northeastern coast of the Sandreach from the mainland to the north. The straits take their name from the massive dust storms that blow off the Luquihn Desert and sweep across the water, reducing visibility to near zero and coating ships in layers of fine sand.

name: Sarki Tulo
content:
The only significant settlement in Kirthdron Soytas. Sarki Tulo was a support village for harvesting operations in the deeper mountains. It sits abandoned now, like all the southern sites—intact, empty, waiting.

Some expedition proposals have focused on Sarki Tulo specifically, arguing that its position at the edge of the contaminated zone might make it safer to explore. The priesthood has rejected all such proposals. The contamination doesn't respect convenient boundaries.

name: Saron
content: Deeper into Grimreach, Saron is the last settlement before the Darkened Coast. The town exists to process and trade the strange materials that come out of the shadow-touched regions: void-eel oil, shadow-ray leather, and darker things. Saron's merchants ask no questions about provenance and expect none about destination.

name: Scar
content:
Tags: dragon, creature

A brownish dragon laired in the Myjornis Mountains. Scar is named for the distinctive marking across its flank—whether from an old wound or natural coloration, none can say. The dragon is territorial but not particularly aggressive by draconic standards, content to extract tribute from travelers crossing the mountain passes rather than hunting them outright.

Scar's presence has shaped travel patterns through the Myjornis range for generations. Caravans factor "dragon tax" into their costs, and experienced guides know which routes are within Scar's claimed territory.

name: Scatter Sound
content:
Tags: sea, geography

The body of water between the Eskiloko coast, the Tyvern Isles, and the Isle of the Night. Named for the countless small islands, rocks, and reefs scattered across its surface, Scatter Sound is a maze of hazards and hiding places that rewards local knowledge and punishes overconfidence.

The sound isn't deep — much of it can be waded at low tide if you know where to step. This makes it navigable for small craft but treacherous for larger vessels, whose keels find the bottom unexpectedly. The scattered islands break up waves and currents, creating calm patches next to sudden turbulence.

name: Scheleur River
content:
The Scheleur rises somewhere in the jungle's interior—the Neka claim to know its source but don't share that information—and flows southwest before curving west to empty into the sea south of Urok's main landmass. For most of its length it's a typical jungle river: brown water, overhanging vegetation, things with teeth lurking beneath the surface.

But where it passes within a few miles of the Firespine, the Scheleur changes character. The water warms. The color shifts from brown to a faintly reddish tinge (mineral content from the heated groundwater, probably, though the Neka have other explanations). The species living in this stretch are found nowhere else—adapted to temperatures that would kill their relatives downstream.

Yanayazi claims stewardship of the warm stretch, but the river is technically accessible to all Neka. Fishing rights are governed by informal traditions rather than strict law, which occasionally causes friction when a Kanamazi hunting party takes fish that Yanayazi considers theirs. These disputes rarely escalate beyond angry words—the confederacy has larger concerns—but they're a persistent minor irritation.

The river is navigable by small craft from Yanayazi to the coast. Larger vessels can't handle the shifting channels and submerged debris. Foreign cartographers named it the Scheleur; the Neka simply call it *Yolu-Mazi*, "fire's vein."

name: Schousterwood
content: The eastern forest, closer to the coast. Slightly younger growth, more connected to the outside world through coastal trade (what little exists).

name: Sculpin Islands
content:
Tags: island, geography

A group of islands off the Seacleft Coast.

TODO

name: Seabreach Forest
content: Southern coastal forest, along the approach to the Telphineas Strait. Strategically important—whoever controls this coastline controls access to the strait from the north.

name: Seatower Island
content:
West of the main island, **Seatower** rises from a rocky spire overlooking the main shipping lanes. The fortress commands views in every direction—nothing approaches without being spotted.

The island is smaller and steeper than the main island, with almost no flat ground. Buildings cling to the rock face, connected by stairs and rope bridges. The harbor is barely a notch in the cliffs, accessible only in calm weather.

**Notable Features:**
- **The Watch**: An observation platform at the fortress's peak, staffed day and night
- **The War Room**: A vast chamber with sand tables, ship models, and maps covering every known coast
- **Signal Towers**: A network of fire signals connecting Seatower to the other fortresses

name: Seavault
content: A northern coastal town with a natural harbor protected by rocky headlands. The name comes from sea caves in the cliffs where, according to legend, pirates once hid treasure. Whether any treasure remains is anyone's guess.

name: Seaview
content: A settlement on Tykos's southeastern edge, overlooking open water. Seaview maintains the lighthouse network that guides ships through the island chain's treacherous passages—a service that earns goodwill even from nations that despise Tykos's primary trade.

name: Secret Mountains
content:
Tags: mountains

A small range of mountains south of Windor, where a halfling infamously kept secret a massive goldmine in the hills for nearly a decade. The gold has long been mined out and the mines abandoned, but the story remains legend.

name: Serisa's Palace
content:
Tags: ruins, dungeon, cursed, naval, kraken

The crumbling fortress of a pirate queen who made a bargain with the deep—and whose heir broke it.

name: Seron Lake
content:
The middle of the three western lakes, smaller than Sethlund but deeper. Something lives in Seron Lake's depths—something large enough to create waves on windless days and old enough that even the hags of Hagswood treat it with respect.

The creature (if it is singular) has never been clearly seen. Fishermen in the time before the empire reported glimpses of scaled coils, massive shadows passing beneath their boats. Now, with the lake abandoned, it presumably feeds on whatever falls or is thrown in. The hags are suspected of making offerings.

name: Sethlund Lake
content:
The largest of the three western lakes near Hagswood, Sethlund has become hag territory. The coven uses its waters for rituals, its shores for camps, and its depths for disposing of victims. Fishing boats haven't worked these waters in decades.

The lake's surface often carries an oily sheen—residue from hag magic or something fouler. Locals claim the water itself has become poisonous, that anyone who drinks it falls under the hags' influence. True or not, no one tests the theory voluntarily.

name: Sha'ra
content: A port town specializing in overland caravan trade—the route across the Dustwind Plateau to the Taflex Forest and beyond passes through here. Sha'ra maintains caravanserais, stables, and the guides necessary to survive the plateau crossing. The harbor serves coastal trade, but the real money flows through the caravan yards.

name: Shad Usk
content:
The largest and most treacherous dune formation in central Kura, Shad Usk rises nearly three hundred feet at its peak and stretches for miles in a crescent shape. The dune moves—slowly, but perceptibly over years—and has swallowed more than one unwary settlement that built too close to its base.

Shad Usk is considered sacred by some Kurans, who believe the dune is alive in some sense, or that something sleeps beneath it. The sand wurms that plague the Dunes of Kunagi avoid Shad Usk entirely, which some take as confirmation of this belief.

Local guides know the safe paths around Shad Usk, but crossing over it is considered both dangerous (the sand is unstable) and disrespectful.

name: Shalif Mulak
content:
A village of perhaps two thousand souls, located half a day's travel from Bani Bari on the western side of the ley line. Shalif Mulak serves as the agricultural heart of central Kura—its underground aquifers, maintained by Nuun water-shapers, support date palms, hardy grains, and goat herds that would otherwise be impossible in the deep desert.

The village is known for its hospitality to travelers. Anyone heading into the deep dunes stops here for supplies and the last reliable water before Benshu's Promise.

name: Shambling Forest
content: On the eastern slopes of the Tambrielle Mountains lies the Shambling Forest, a dense woodland with an unsettling reputation. The forest earned its name from travelers' reports of trees that seem to move when unobserved—paths shift, landmarks relocate, and those who enter often emerge miles from where they expected. Whether this is natural disorientation, magical influence, or something stranger remains disputed.

name: Shamwet
content: A tree-settlement in the northern part of Stipen, near the Chedwin Strait coast.

name: Shananae
content:
A dragon of the Northlands.

TODO

name: Shargon Hills
content:
Tags: hills, geography

Hills in the Dragon's Spine Coast.

TODO

name: Shark Bay
content:
Tags: water, geography

A broad, shallow bay on Westrim's southwestern coast, providing Atriik's only maritime access. The bay takes its name from the shark populations that patrol its waters—a hazard for swimmers and small boats, but manageable for proper vessels.

name: Sharvatunis Fortress
content:
The eastern guardian of the Tarkhon Passage, watching the approaches from the Middle Sea.

Sharvatunis sits on Astrelle's eastern coast, positioned to intercept any fleet approaching from the east before it reaches the Needle. It's the first line of defense against naval threats from the Middle Sea kingdoms.

**Garrison**: ~300 soldiers, professional but not elite. Adequate equipment, adequate training. Good enough for a posting that rarely sees action.

**Posting**: Sharvatunis is neither prestigious nor punishing—it's simply a solid, respectable assignment. Officers serve their terms, do their jobs, and move on. Nothing exciting happens, but nothing terrible either.

**Commander**: **Captain Mira Solent**, a career officer in her forties. Competent, unambitious, and content to run a well-maintained fortress until retirement.

name: Sherly & Lieler
content: Small settlements deeper still, barely more than camps. These serve as waypoints for those trading with the Yngli communities or venturing toward Grimreach. Not places to linger.

name: Shimmer Lake
content:
**Shimmer Lake** lies at The Glog's eastern edge, marking the boundary between forest and the foothills of The Screech. The lake earns its name from the way light plays across its surface—reflections that seem too bright, too active, as if the water itself generates luminescence.

The phenomenon is probably optical, caused by mineral content refracting sunlight in unusual ways. Or it might be magical. The distinction matters because Shimmer Lake is Pelera's lair.

The green dragon has claimed the lake and surrounding territory as her own. The shimmer may be natural, may be a side effect of draconic presence, or may be something Pelera deliberately cultivates. She doesn't explain, and no one asks. What's known is that the lake's boundaries define the core of her territory—anyone entering without her sufferance is either brave, desperate, or unaware of their danger.

The lake supposedly contains a submerged structure of some kind—glimpsed occasionally when the angle of light is right. Whether this is Pelera's actual lair, a pre-existing ruin she's claimed, or a trick of the shimmer effect is unknown.

name: Shimmercreek Falls
content: A town built around a dramatic waterfall where a river tumbles off the northern highlands. The falls are genuinely beautiful—one of the few places in Sylke where people might visit for pleasure rather than business. Mills powered by the falls produce flour and lumber.

name: Shinishini
content: Interior city-state known for its performers. Traveling Shinishini theater troupes occasionally venture into Erasnus and beyond, staging elaborate productions that blend comedy, tragedy, and chaos.

name: Shipmarsh
content: The largest city in Adron after Adrak, built where the Tenori River meets the sea. Massive shipyards produce vessels for the Middle Sea trade. A working city—less refined than Adrak, more prosperous than anywhere else.

name: Shipton
content:
The capital—if a town of a few thousand can claim that title. Shipton clusters around the best natural harbor on the island, a sheltered bay on the western coast facing Ofrenia. The waterfront is busy with transient ships; the inland streets are quiet and suspicious.

The governor's residence is the largest building in town, a weathered stone structure that once served as a warehouse. Governors come and go—Alisandra rotates them frequently, ensuring no one builds a power base—but the bureaucracy grinds on regardless.

The prison complex sprawls along the northern edge of town, repurposed warehouses surrounded by walls that look impressive but wouldn't stop a determined escape. The prisoners don't try because there's nowhere to go. Swimming to Ofrenia is suicide; stowing away on a ship requires bribing sailors who'd rather collect the reward for turning you in.

name: Shipwreck Hills
content:
West of Gyosha lie the Shipwreck Hills—a series of islands whose hills are dotted with the remains of countless wrecked vessels. Ships destroyed by the nearby Shardmaw Isles wash ashore here, their broken hulls littering the landscape like tombstones in a maritime graveyard.

The hills are inhospitable not just because of the treacherous terrain, but because they are home to rusalka and wyrms. The wyrms—serpentine creatures that once roamed the crust of the world as titans the size of mountain ranges—have diminished over the ages. Now they rarely exceed a thousand feet in length, but they are still formidable predators. The combination of predatory wyrms and deadly water spirits makes the Shipwreck Hills a place to avoid, despite the potential salvage opportunities among the wrecks.

name: Shredwreck
content:
Tags: shipwreck, poi, salvage

The scattered remains of the *Golden Sovereignty*, a treasure galleon that went down in the waters near the Golem's Teeth three generations ago. Unlike most shipwrecks, Shredwreck isn't a single location — the ship was torn apart by the rocks and currents, its cargo scattered across more than a mile of seafloor in dozens of separate debris fields.

The treasure is real. The *Golden Sovereignty* was carrying the accumulated wealth of a failed merchant house — a final desperate voyage to relocate their fortune before creditors could seize it. Gold, silver, gems, trade goods, and reportedly several items of magical significance went down with the ship. Enough has been recovered over the decades to prove the stories true. Enough remains to keep treasure hunters coming.

name: Shtaneres
content: The ruins of an ancient city in Breia, built at the intersection of fire and air leylines. The city was destroyed long ago, its structures reduced to rubble by magical catastrophe.

name: Shyalaviin (Malasi Karkus)
content: Hills just south of the Eronia range that poke into the northern edge of the Dunes of Kunagi. This region, also known as Malasi Karkus, is home to the Blitzling—creatures that inhabit these transitional hills between the mountains and the desert sands.

name: Shyonn
content: A southwestern settlement near the border with Eoga. Shyonn has historically been contested territory—relations between Vystrilik and Eoga are complicated, and the town has changed hands multiple times.

name: Shystani Forest
content: A small forest east of Eidlesandres, notable for its sacred sites and ancient power.

name: Simonsor
content: Major city in the north, near the Hephake mountain passes. Trade routes through the Kharvorn run through here.

name: Simphiwe
content:
The western city, gateway to Ntando. Simphiwe sits at the edge of the great forests, serving as the commercial bridge between the traditional interior and the coastal economy. This is the most Qindo of the cities — foreign merchants come here to buy Ntando goods, but they don't dominate the way they do in Mjiqa.

The architecture blends coral-city style with traditional Ntando woodwork. The markets sell forest products alongside imported luxuries. The voting rings matter here, but so do older forms of respect — a ring-holder who insults local customs finds their business mysteriously difficult.

Simphiwe has the largest urban halfling population, many maintaining connections to the destroyed cave-homes in the nearby mountains. The halfling district is culturally significant; ceremonies commemorating the lost caves happen here, attended by halflings from across Iqes.

name: Sirm al Banna
content: A village on a tributary stream, Sirm al Banna is known for its date palms—the finest in the Sal'emri region. The village processes and exports preserved dates, date wine, and date syrup throughout the settlements. Small, wealthy, and deeply uninterested in politics beyond its orchards.

name: Sivirine
content: The second city of Ethadia occupies the archipelago's northwest coast, facing the open Gindrik rather than the sheltered waters of the main harbor. Approximately fifteen thousand people live here—sailors, fishers, and those who prefer distance from Regina's politics.

name: Skoldar
content: A quarry town on a rocky island whose stone has been extracted for generations. Slave labor works the quarries under minotaur overseers, cutting blocks that are shipped across the Middle Sea. The work is hard and dangerous; life expectancy in Skoldar's quarries is not long.

name: Skyloft Castle
content:
Tags: ruins, poi

Ruined castle within Grustos.

TODO

name: Skyreach
content:
A fortress carved into a high mountain saddle, built in a style that predates the Sylthik city-states by centuries. No one knows who constructed it or why they chose such an inaccessible location. The structure is largely intact but abandoned—reaching it requires days of climbing through treacherous terrain, and whatever value it once held apparently wasn't worth the effort of maintaining supply lines.

Scholars have noted that Skyreach sits almost exactly where multiple mountain passes converge, suggesting it once controlled movement through the Aktarvkus. Some speculate it was a toll fortress from an earlier civilization; others think it served as a watchtower or signal station. The Sylthik occasionally use it as a neutral meeting ground for inter-city negotiations, precisely because no single city-state can easily garrison it.

name: Skywatch
content:
Tags: ruins, poi

Skywatch is an ancient observatory perched near the summit of one of the tallest peaks in the Kharvorn Mountains, on the northern edge of the Blueshale range. The peak itself—unnamed on most maps, simply "the great mountain"—rises so high that its summit is perpetually shrouded in cloud, and the air at its heights is too thin for most creatures to breathe.

name: Slavewatch
content:
Tags: fortress, poi, tamadrez, shacklands

A Gorathi fortress carved into the central Freedom Mountains, overlooking the Slaver's Coast and the shipping lanes that connect the slave ports to Divinity Passage. Slavewatch was built to monitor the mountain passes and prevent escaped slaves from reaching the northern coast. After two centuries, it has become something else entirely: a corrupt institution that profits from both sides of the trade it ostensibly polices.

name: Slavos
content:
The capital and largest settlement, built around the primary slave market. Slavos sits on the largest island's best harbor, positioned to receive raiding ships returning with captives and merchant vessels arriving to buy.

The market operates daily, with major auctions weekly. The facility is well-organized, almost clinical—holding pens, inspection areas, auction blocks, and processing centers for new arrivals. The minotaurs treat their merchandise as valuable property, which means adequate food, basic medical care, and protection from damage. It's not kindness; it's business.

The ruling clan of Slavos holds outsized influence in Tykos politics by controlling the market. Other clans raid and capture; Slavos processes and sells. The arrangement creates dependencies that keep the peace.

name: Slickrun
content:
The westernmost river descends from the Miramar Peaks through a channel of metamorphic stone polished to an almost glassy smoothness by millennia of meltwater. The Slickrun earns its name from this stone—and from the speed of the current, which moves far faster than its placid surface suggests.

Travelers who attempt to ford the Slickrun often don't survive the experience. The smooth riverbed offers no purchase, and what looks like knee-deep water can sweep a person off their feet and carry them half a mile before depositing them against a rock or into the strait. Local guides mark safe crossing points with cairns, but the markers shift seasonally as the river's channel migrates.

The Slickrun's mouth opens into a relatively calm bay on the western edge of the strait, where the fishing village of Polished Stone has grown up around the pilot trade. The villagers have developed a unique method of "skating" the river using flat-bottomed sledges, riding the current down from the mountains in a fraction of the time it takes to climb back up.

name: Smokestack
content:
The volcanic peak at New Chimea's southern border, shared with Vystrilik. Smokestack perpetually vents steam and sulfurous gases—on clear days, the plume is visible from 50 miles at sea, serving as a navigation landmark for ships approaching the coast.

The volcano is active but not violently so—minor eruptions occur every few decades, producing lava flows that rarely threaten settled areas. The volcanic soil in the surrounding lowlands is extraordinarily fertile. A settlement of the same name clings to the mountain's slopes, processing sulfur and volcanic minerals.

name: Snakefoot Hills
content:
Tags: hills, geography

Hills within the Grendenheim region.

TODO

name: Snøvgar (Village)
content:
Population: ~200

A fishing village north of Svedlind, clinging to the coast where Murder Creek empties into the Void. The villagers are hardened by proximity to both cursed water and open sea. They catch silverfin in waters other fishermen avoid, claiming the fish here taste better—touched by the creek's strange power without being poisoned by it.

name: Sobrevire
content:
Tags: forest, geography

A forest in Central Aboyinzu.

TODO

name: Solis
content:
A marked location on the remote northeast peninsula, deep in jungle that grows differently than the rest of the island. The pods here glow amber instead of purple-green. The trees twist into older, stranger shapes. The Velwey don't go to Solis. They don't forbid it, exactly. They just don't discuss it.

**What's There**: Ruins. Structures that predate the Velwey, built by something that understood true names before the dwarves arrived. The Velwey founding stories say they "discovered" the Pool and "were called" to Movasi. Solis suggests a different history—that something was here first.

**The Seekers**: Occasionally a pilgrim, unsatisfied with what the Pool revealed (or having received no answer at all), makes the trek to Solis. Most don't return. The few who do seem changed—not damaged, necessarily. Just different. They don't talk about what they found.

**The Rumor**: Something lives at Solis. Something with a true name that cannot be spoken by mortal mouths—a name that predates language itself. Whether this is metaphor or literal, no one outside the peninsula knows.

The Velwey do not discuss Solis with pilgrims. Questions about it are politely deflected. Insistence is met with silence.

Population: Unknown.

name: Sookap
content: A fishing village where the Sovia widens into lazy pools. Sookap's fishers harvest river-creatures for food and ritual use, including the sacred blood-eels whose consumption is required for High Priestess initiation. The village also maintains the ritual boats used to send offerings downstream.

name: Sopana Strempele
content:
Tags: lake, geography

A lake within Grustos.

TODO

name: Sopo Calisando River
content: A southern river draining the Bolsaña P'Suelos forest toward the coast. The Calisando is shallow, slow, and warm—poor for transport but excellent for irrigation. The farms along its banks produce most of Grustos's grain.

name: Sopona Fia
content:
Tags: lake, geography

A lake within Grustos.

TODO

name: Sopona Strempele
content:
A smaller lake in the central region, near the town of Opela Sopona. Unlike its larger cousin, Sopona Strempele welcomes visitors—fishing boats work its waters, and swimming is a popular summer activity.

Local legend claims the two Sopona lakes were once connected, separated when a sun elf priest raised the land between them to create privacy for Enuau Kemjallb. The geological evidence is ambiguous.

name: Sothills
content:
Tags: hills, geography

Hills in Central Aboyinzu.

TODO

name: South Wall
content:
Tags: mountains, geography

A wall in the Thundering Mountains.

TODO

name: Sova Chyda
content: Istora's lifeline. This clear river flows from springs in the Suftos Hills through the Winterwood and past Tuur to the sea. The Istori have built their civilization along its banks—it provides drinking water, irrigation, and transport. Unlike Murder Creek, its waters are pure. The elves say this proves their faction's righteousness.

name: Sovia River
content:
The Sovia winds through the heart of Zelidian territory, flowing northeast from the jungle interior toward the coast. All five Zelidian settlements cluster along its banks, connected by the river's dark waters. The Zelle travel it by leaf-boat and wing, and its currents carry offerings downstream to wherever the jungle-spirits wish to receive them.

Outsiders sometimes attempt to navigate the Sovia to reach Zelidian markets. Most are intercepted within hours. The fortunate are merely turned away. The rest feed the river's hungry gods.

name: Soya
content: A small village in the agricultural interior, unremarkable except for its beans. Soya produces a particular variety of legume that grows nowhere else in Qintas, forming the basis of several traditional dishes. The village is proud of its beans. Visitors are expected to admire them.

name: Spire of Ascension
content:
Tags: poi

A giant pinnacle on a massive, ornate base. Must perform hefty sacrifice and know your own true name, but takes you directly outside the Alarian planar stack.

name: Splintercrests
content:
Tags: hills, geography

Hills in Wycendeula.

TODO

name: Starfall
content:
At the center of Xabraedia lies Starfall—a pool of intense, otherworldly energy unlike anything else on Alaria. The pool contains starblood, a substance that glows with a deep, alien purple-red color and twinkles with inner lights. This energy comes from a distant planar stack, having naturally coalesced here in a phenomenon that defies normal magical understanding.

The starblood is the remnant of a massive sky worm that escaped its home plane of Glyssen and came to Alaria, where it was promptly disintegrated by a coalition of titans. The creature's blood pooled here, barely contained on this plane. When removed from the pool, the substance begins teleporting back toward Glyssen—it cannot exist far from the pool's location.

Sometimes, people who enter the pool never come back, drawn into the alien energy or pulled toward the distant plane from which the starblood originates. The pool remains a site of mystery, danger, and otherworldly power.

name: Starreach
content: A tower that extends all the way from the ground to the Astral plane itself. The valkyries of Solhalven have been cursed to guard Starreach from the angels Sarakiel and Xaphkiel, and they are bound to permit only certain chosen individuals to ascend the tower. The curse has transformed what might have been an honor into an eternal duty, and the valkyries stand watch over this bridge between worlds, never knowing rest.

name: Statue of Feithon
content:
Tags: poi

TODO

name: Steed Hills
content:
A rocky highland in the west where the Gray Citadel stands—one of the last holdout fortresses against the undead empire. The hills were once famous for horse-breeding, producing cavalry mounts prized across Terrenia. That tradition is extinct now; the horses were eaten during sieges or taken by the empire.

The terrain favors defense. Rocky outcrops, narrow passes, and poor footing make the Steed Hills difficult for shambling undead armies to traverse. The Gray Citadel was built here precisely because the land itself resists assault. So far, it has held.

name: Stillstone
content: A fortress in the mountains, exact purpose unclear.

name: Stillwater
content: Within Ámas Kiori, north of Adrak in the central basin. A sacred pool guarded by Naga priests where time bends—eddies where time races, and a still center where it stops entirely. Located near the intersection of the water and time ley lines.

name: Stonebreach
content: A coastal fortress town on Tykos's eastern edge, positioned to watch the Tykon Channel and the ongoing chaos in Ofrenia. Stonebreach serves as early warning for any naval threat and as a base for raids into disrupted Ofrenian waters.

name: Stormfather
content:
In the Blueshale Peaks dwells Stormfather, an earth-attuned cyclops of tremendous power. Before Argysis fell to the dragon Rothogomos, Stormfather served as a counselor to the Argysisean dwarves, offering wisdom and aid in times of need.

Stormfather remains in the peaks, a powerful ally to those who can earn his favor. He will trade his help for gems and stories of the world—the more compelling the tale, the more willing he is to assist. Alternatively, those brave (or foolish) enough can challenge him to an arm-wrestling match. Victory earns his respect and aid, though few possess the strength to best a cyclops of his might.

name: Straker
content: A dragon on Glyn'Qoth.

name: Stulov River
content:
The central river, smaller than the Chechik but supporting the most productive farmland. The Stulov Valley is Chechol's breadbasket—wide, flat, and intensively cultivated.

**Major Settlements:**
- **Stulovka** (~7,000): The valley's main town, a prosperous agricultural center known for grain markets.
- **Yeletity** (~4,000): A smaller town upriver, increasingly dominated by a nearby Rentar monastery.

name: Sunbeam River
content:
The central river flows down from the Silver Slopes, and its name requires no explanation to anyone who sees it at midday. The riverbed is dense with mica, pyrite, and actual silver flake—runoff from the mining operations around Sweivyon. When the sun hits the water at the right angle, the entire river blazes with reflected light, visible for miles.

Sweivyon has built its economy partly on controlling Sunbeam River access. The city-state operates a series of sluice gates and settling pools upstream, capturing the heavier metals before they wash out to sea. What escapes the sluices supports a secondary economy of "glitter-panners"—independent prospectors who work the lower river and strait-mouth, recovering what Sweivyon's filters miss.

The Sunbeam is navigable for shallow-draft vessels up to about fifteen miles inland, where rapids begin. A portage road connects the navigable lower river to Sweivyon proper, facilitating the silver trade. The river-mouth settlement of Glintwater serves as Sweivyon's de facto port.

name: Sunshine Meadows
content:
Tags: plains, geography, free-isles, dungeon

Cursed meadowy hills on the southern islands of the Free Isles, where the sun burns brighter than it should and the creatures have learned to use that light as a weapon. Sunshine Meadows is home to four significant points of interest—ancient tombs and strange structures that draw treasure-seekers, scholars, and fools from across Alaria.

name: Surrey Mahaila
content:
Tags: dragon, creature

A dragon of Westrim.

TODO

name: Survivor's Island
content:
At the center of the Sea of Sharks lies **Survivor's Island**—home to the city-state of Teku. The inhabitants are descendants of refugees whose ships were destroyed here; they couldn't leave, so they learned to survive.

Teku has developed techniques for existing in shark waters:
- Metal-reinforced hulls that resist shark teeth
- Chemical compounds that discourage shark approach
- Trained shark-hunters who control the population around the harbor
- Knowledge of shark behavior that lets them predict relatively safe windows

Reaching Teku requires crossing shark-infested waters. Most ships hire Teku pilots who know the patterns; others simply sail fast and hope.

name: Sweetwoods
content:
The northernmost forest in Phirexes, a relatively mundane woodland of sweetgum and cypress that provides most of Maurevelious's timber. Dengar woodcutters work here in rotating crews, harvesting lumber for construction and fuel.

"Relatively mundane" is a low bar in Phirexes. Even here, the fog has seeped in. Workers report hearing voices in the trees—conversations just below the threshold of comprehension, as if the forest were gossiping about them. Shadows move wrong, lingering a moment after their casters have passed. Tools go missing and reappear in strange places.

The queen maintains a logging camp at Sweetwoods' edge, more for surveillance than efficiency. Any woodcutter who spends too long in the forest alone is quietly reassigned. Whatever the trees are saying, Phendexelas doesn't want her subjects to hear it.

name: Swindle Sound
content:
Southeast of the Nekanzi Jungle, where the coastline curves inward, lies the body of water that foreign sailors call Swindle Sound. The name comes from bitter experience: treacherous currents, hidden reefs, and sandbars that seem to shift between voyages have wrecked dozens of ships over the centuries. Captains who thought they'd found a shortcut around Tyunigogo's tolls discovered instead why the Neka don't bother patrolling these waters.

The Neka call it *Zend-Kulam*, roughly "the patient water"—patient because it waits for ships to make mistakes, then collects them. They don't fish here; the currents make small craft as vulnerable as large ones. Occasionally valuable salvage washes up on the jungle's southeastern beaches, but more often it's just wreckage and bodies.

Smugglers still try. Some even succeed—there are channels through the sound that skilled pilots can navigate, though the knowledge is closely held and expensive. The Neka are aware of this traffic and largely ignore it. Anything that makes it through Swindle Sound and into the jungle still has to deal with the jungle, and the Neka would rather let the sound do the filtering than waste hunters on patrol duty.

**Hooks:**
- A ship carrying something valuable went down in the sound—someone might pay well for its recovery
- A smuggler claims to know a safe route through and is selling passage
- Something in the sound has started actively hunting ships rather than just waiting for wrecks

name: Swinehead Forest
content:
At the southern edge of the Sorgül, where the mountains descend toward the Breidleheis basin, lies **Swinehead Forest**—a dense, ancient woodland named for the enormous boars that roam beneath its canopy.

The boars of Swinehead are massive—the smallest are pony-sized, the largest rival oxen. They are aggressive, territorial, and extraordinarily dangerous. For the Güli giants, they are sacred prey.

A young giant's first solo boar kill marks their passage to adulthood. The hunt is undertaken alone, with traditional weapons only—no magic, no assistance, no retreat. Success earns the tusks, which are carved into ritual objects worn for life. Failure means death or shame that never fades.

Lowlanders occasionally poach in Swinehead. The giants usually ignore this—humans hunting pigs is beneath notice. But anyone who kills a giant's marked prey, or worse, a breeding sow protected by clan law, faces summary execution. No trial, no appeal. Just a giant's boot.

name: Sygozoki
content:
*"Burning Without End"*

Southern Sygozoki is the forge-village, built directly on the Firespine at a point where the fissure's heat is most consistent and controllable. This is where the Neka make things—tools, weapons, art—using techniques that blend conventional smithing with fire-spirit negotiation.

Sygozoki's forges don't use bellows. They don't need to. Fire-speakers maintain ongoing agreements with spirits bound to each forge, ensuring steady, controllable heat that mundane fuel couldn't match. The smiths—called *char-hands* for the permanent discoloration their work leaves—shape metal in temperatures that would kill an unprotected worker, relying on their racial fire resistance and additional spirit-bargains for protection.

The results are distinctive. Sygozoki metalwork has a rippled, flame-pattern finish that can't be replicated elsewhere, and the metal itself holds heat longer than it should. Weapons made here don't burn their wielders, but they burn everything else—a Sygozoki blade drawn across flesh leaves cauterized wounds. The most valued pieces have spirits bound into them permanently, granting minor magical properties that vary by the smith and the spirits involved.

This is also the most dangerous village for outsiders. The spirits here are more active, more present, more *hungry* than those at Hyhi. Sygozoki's fire-speakers maintain control through constant negotiation and regular offerings, but the balance is precarious. Visitors who don't understand the protocols—who carry too much flammable material, who speak disrespectfully of fire, who simply look like they might be good fuel—tend to attract attention. The villagers will try to intervene. They don't always succeed.

Sygozoki has burned twice in living memory. Both times, the village was rebuilt within a year, slightly differently, slightly more attuned to the spirits' preferences. The Neka here accept this as part of their arrangement. They get to work with fire in ways no one else can. Sometimes fire works with them back, in ways they didn't intend. The forge-gift and the forge-price are the same thing.

**Hooks:**
- Sygozoki weapons are valuable trade goods, but acquiring one requires negotiating with the char-hands directly
- A smith has bound something into a blade that they shouldn't have, and now the blade is missing
- The spirits have been restless lately—demanding more offerings, honoring bargains less reliably

name: Sygum River
content: Southeastern river connecting Kessus Lake to the coast. Sy maintains watchtowers along its length, the closest thing to a safe corridor through the eastern jungle. Even so, Ulvsjael raids hit the river settlements regularly.

name: Tacuarembo
content: A city in the agricultural heartland, surrounded by the farms that feed Grustos. Tacuarembo is quietly proud of being boring—no sun elves, no goblins, no giants, just humans growing food and minding their business. Visitors from the more dramatic parts of the state often find it restful.

name: Tafleur Wood
content:
Tags: forest, geography

A forest on Phenol.

TODO

name: Tanim Oscurus
content:
The western reaches of the Ngül overlook a region the giants call **Tanim Oscurus**—the Dark Grazing. This area, sloping down toward the lowlands, was once giant territory. Three centuries ago, something drove the clans out. The giants do not speak of what happened, but they no longer graze their herds there, and young giants are forbidden from descending the western slopes alone.

Lowlanders who have explored Tanim Oscurus report finding it unremarkable—empty grassland, a few ruins, nothing obviously wrong. But they also report feeling watched, and a persistent sense that they should leave before nightfall.

name: Tasah Evikktus River
content:
A southwestern river notable primarily for what it passes: the ruins of Skyloft Castle. The river's name predates the castle's fall, suggesting it was significant before the fortress was built—or that the fortress was built because of the river's significance.

---

name: Taufig
content: Southern town, near the border with Kazül. Handles most of the (deliberately modest) gold exports.

name: Tel Askati
content: A settlement in southwestern Qet Yemani, near the Kos Askati river and the approaches to the Myjornis Mountains. Tel Askati marks the edge of jungle civilization, where the lowland forests give way to mountain terrain.

name: Telara
content: A secondary port on the eastern coast, smaller and rougher than Shipton. Ships that want to avoid attention—smugglers, pirates looking to fence goods, merchants carrying questionable cargo—often prefer Telara's lack of oversight. The town's unofficial motto is "we don't ask."

name: Teldib
content:
The great port on the Bay of Roule, Teldib is the commercial and naval heart of southern Roule. The harbor can accommodate hundreds of vessels; the naval yards employ thousands. This is where Roule's military power is built, maintained, and deployed.

The city divides into three distinct zones: the Naval Quarter (restricted, bristling with uniforms and checkpoints), the Merchant District (chaotic, profitable, smelling of fish and spices), and the Estates (quiet, wealthy, overlooking the bay). The Sunbeam Prison is visible from anywhere in Teldib, a constant reminder of what happens to those who cross Roulean authority.

Teldib's admiral is technically subordinate to the Parliament of Flowers, but in practice the naval command operates with considerable autonomy. The current admiral, a halfling woman named Tessara Brightwater, is said to have ambitions beyond the military.

name: Teleriwood
content: The western forest, bordering Thespia. Older, denser, with the most ancient trees. This is where the deepest parts of Stipen lie—and where the controversial psychedelic flora grows.

name: Temple of Bryn
content:
Located in the foothills north of Belu Jenari, at the intersection of the fire and light leylines, the Temple of Bryn was once a magnificent structure dedicated to Bryn, the sun-god. The temple featured extremely bright eternal flames and a brilliant artificial sun at its center, channeling the raw elemental power of the leyline convergence.

The temple was destroyed during the formation of Belu Jenari itself—the volcanic eruption that created the mountain also devastated the temple, leaving only ruins. However, one artifact survived the cataclysm.

name: Temple of the Sun
content:
Tags: poi, ruin

An ancient temple complex in the central Luquihn Desert, south of the Dunes of Kalamesh. Unlike the destroyed Temple of Bryn near Belu Jenari, this temple remains largely intact—its builders constructed it from massive blocks of white stone that have resisted centuries of sandstorms.

The temple is dedicated to sun worship, though scholars debate which tradition built it. The architecture predates known human settlement in the region, and the carvings show stylized sun imagery that doesn't match any contemporary religious iconography. Some theorize it was built by an early Murazi surface civilization before they retreated underground; others attribute it to a forgotten human culture.

The interior remains sealed. The main entrance is blocked by a stone door covered in worn glyphs that have resisted all attempts at translation. Treasure hunters have tried to breach the walls, but the white stone is unnaturally hard—picks and chisels barely scratch it. Whatever secrets the Temple of the Sun holds, it guards them jealously.

name: Temple of the Wind
content:
High in the Mountains Semaphois stands the wind-racked, abandoned Temple of the Wind. This remote structure was once home to monks and primeshapers who mastered both earth and air magic, using the mountain winds to fuel their practice. The temple was destroyed when a mechwurm attacked, killing the monks and leaving the temple empty.

Now the temple stands as a haunting reminder, its halls filled with the howling winds that once served the monks' magic. The structure itself remains intact, though battered by decades of relentless wind, and the knowledge the monks possessed has been lost with their deaths.

name: Temple of Zicadia
content:
At the northern tip of the Wurmling Mountains, where force and air leylines intersect, stands the Temple of Zicadia. This remote institution is dedicated to elemental protection magic—specifically, the art of turning air into solid structures that can deflect attacks.

The temple is run by Master Felwana, a reclusive woman who lost her husband in a distant war and came to the Northlands to learn. She became a master of the art and now teaches others, though the temple is brutal and miserable. Life here is harsh—the temple faces frequent threats from the creatures of the frozen snowplains, and students must be prepared to defend themselves while mastering the difficult discipline of solidifying air itself.

name: Ternor
content: A point of interest in the desert.

name: Terror Grove
content:
Something went wrong in Terror Grove.

The forest between Spinefir and Hagwood was once ordinary woodland. Now it's avoided. The trees grow twisted. Animals don't enter. Gyv who venture in report unease that escalates to panic, whispers they can't quite hear, the absolute certainty that something is watching.

The Gyv don't talk about what happened in Terror Grove. Whatever it was, it was recent—within living memory—and important enough to suppress. Some say an experiment in mass manipulation went wrong. Others say something was summoned. Others say it's simply where the Gyv disposed of problems they didn't want found.

No one goes to Terror Grove unless they have to. Those who do come back changed—or don't come back at all.

name: Terwin Lake
content:
A lake in the Dalizi Wildlands.

TODO

name: Tezina
content:
The city closest to Murderwood, Tezina exists in the forest's shadow—sometimes literally, when the afternoon sun falls behind the treeline. The city is smaller than its coastal counterparts and markedly less prosperous. Young halflings from Tezina tend to leave for opportunities elsewhere. Those who remain have their reasons.

Tezina serves as the staging ground for any official business involving the forest—which is to say, almost none. The last "survey expedition" departed fifteen years ago and never returned. The city maintains a token garrison, but the soldiers spend their time drinking and avoiding the forest's edge.

What Tezina does have is information. Travelers, hunters, and those who've glimpsed the forest's secrets pass through here. The city's taverns are full of stories—most of them lies, but occasionally something true slips through. Anyone seriously interested in Murderwood starts their research in Tezina.

name: The Bay of Whispers
content:
The supernatural bay outside Hessam, where the wind carries secrets across time. Everything spoken within the bay is captured forever—words from the past drift across the water, and listeners can hear things they weren't meant to know.

The phenomenon is real and terrifying. A visitor might hear their own voice speaking words they haven't said yet. They might catch fragments of conversations from centuries ago. They might learn secrets that powerful people have killed to protect.

The bay attracts seekers—scholars researching lost knowledge, mystics pursuing temporal communion, the desperate hoping to learn something that might save them or damn their enemies. Hessam's merchants have built an industry around them: lodging, supplies, guides who know the safe approaches (if any approach is truly safe), and discrete disposal services for those who learn things they shouldn't.

The council regulates access but doesn't restrict it. The revenue is too valuable, and besides—trying to control the Bay of Whispers seems like the kind of hubris that ends badly.

name: The Bejeweled Palace
content:
The cursed palace of an ancient Mkinti king, abandoned for centuries. The structure is visible from Tiknurtir on clear days—a gleaming edifice that catches sunlight in ways that hurt to look at.

The palace treasury is legendary. The old king accumulated wealth beyond counting, gems of sizes that shouldn't exist, clockwork marvels that no modern gnome can replicate. He died there, along with his entire court, in circumstances the Ohblex don't discuss. The palace has been avoided ever since.

Something lives in the palace now. Or something remains. Or something waits. The gnomes have stories, each more terrifying than the last, and none of them agree. What everyone agrees on: those who enter don't return. Those who camp too close have nightmares. Those who take souvenirs from the outer grounds regret it.

name: The Birintine River
content:
The Birintine River rises in the central Pinnacles, fed by snowmelt and the numerous springs that emerge from the granite formations. The river flows north through Birintine Gap, passing through the eastern Gaplands before emptying into Birintine Lake at the edge of The Glog.

The river is relatively safe by regional standards—it flows too fast for mermaids, and its course doesn't pass through mermaid-colonized underground systems.
